title: Tweet
body:
## Tweet

MemPalace got 19k stars for retrieval recall metrics. The 96.6% measured ChromaDB's default embeddings, not the palace architecture.

The incentive structure rewarded those numbers. 19,000 stars for retrieval scores, zero questions about write integrity. That is the category's problem, not one project's.

Mem0, Supermemory, Zep. Same axis. Good retrieval is necessary. No benchmark tests what happens to stored data after agents write to it.

No AI memory benchmark tests what actually breaks.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/your-agent-isnt-hallucinating

## LinkedIn

No AI memory benchmark tests what actually breaks.

The metrics that drive adoption in AI memory are all retrieval metrics: recall@k, precision, latency, compression ratio. Good retrieval is necessary. But no widely used benchmark tests what happens to stored data after agents write to it.

MemPalace, Mem0, Supermemory, Zep, LangChain Memory, and dozens of homebrew solutions are competing on the same axis. MemPalace reached 19,000 GitHub stars in two days on retrieval recall scores. The incentive structure that rewarded those numbers, with zero questions about write integrity, is the category's problem, not one project's.

I tracked workouts in ChatGPT for weeks and asked for comparisons to past sessions. The numbers were wrong. I could not tell whether the model hallucinated or the stored data had drifted because the system does not preserve history. That inability to distinguish between the two failure modes is what makes memory corruption harder than hallucination. Corruption passes every hallucination check because the retrieval is correct. The stored data is wrong.

Platform memory (ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini) overwrites on update. Retrieval systems merge or replace memories when they consolidate. Databases can implement event sourcing and audit triggers but almost none do by default. Even smart new designs like Garry Tan's GBrain spec rewrite compiled truth rather than appending to it. The gap is closable. The incentives have not closed it.

Immutability, provenance, and temporal replay prevent silent corruption but carry real costs. Those trade-offs are worth making when agents write state that informs real decisions.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/your-agent-isnt-hallucinating

title: When AI tooling is deflationary (and when it isn't)
excerpt: A founder friend argued that tooling around AI loses value with every new model, and that scraping for pre-validated ideas beats building from conviction. The better distinction is thin wrappers versus durable infrastructure—and complaints in a forum are not the same as validation.
body:
*Idea note from a conversation (March 2026). Not yet a full essay.*

## Working thesis

"AI tooling is deflationary" is half true. **Thin wrappers** (prompt packs, UIs that only expose the latest model) do get competed away as models absorb capability. **Infrastructure** that owns abstractions models do not provide—durable state, versioning, provenance, cross-session integrity—does not deflate on the same curve. Total cost of using AI (tokens plus human direction, review, integration) also does not go to zero; it may rise as people attempt heavier workflows.

A parallel thread: **selection** (find a validated market via research pipelines) versus **conviction** (build from pain you understand). Different failure modes: spreadsheet-discovered ideas you stop caring about in month four, versus products too few people need. "Pre-validated" usually means *signal that a problem is loud*, not that someone will pay, that timing is right, or that you will sustain execution.

## Possible outline

1. What people mean when they say tooling around AI is deflationary (wrapper risk, model release churn).
2. The counter: marginal generation cost is not total cost; token and supervision budgets can grow.
3. Split the category: tooling that wraps the model vs. infrastructure the model cannot replace.
4. Plug-and-play reliability as scarcity when generation is cheap (IKEA vs. custom shop).
5. Short section on validated ideas: complaint mining filters opportunities; it does not replace your own validation loop or answer "will I still care?"
6. Optional tie-in: empirical pull vs. push from real evaluators and design partners, not abstract debate.

## Notes to develop

- Obsolescence risk is the sharper version of "deflationary"—address it directly, not only cost.
- Avoid blurring "software" generically; name the line between wrapper and infrastructure (aligned with Neotoma framing: state integrity, not retrieval quality).
- If naming the interlocutor, keep attribution proportional to a public post (friend / serial builder) unless you clear using a name.

## Related posts

- [I used AI agents to run personalized customer research](/posts/customer-research-through-agents) (when published)
- [What my agentic stack actually does](/posts/what-my-agentic-stack-actually-does)

title: Cybernetics and personal agent infrastructure
excerpt: Norbert Wiener’s steering systems map onto truth, strategy, and execution. Stafford Beer’s viable system model, Ashby’s requisite variety, and second-order feedback explain why durable agent memory isn’t optional for self-governance.
body:
*Idea note from a conversation (March 2026). Not yet a full essay.*

## Working thesis

Cybernetics (control and communication in the animal and the machine) is not a metaphor for agentic personal infrastructure—it is the intellectual lineage. A three-layer stack (truth / strategy / execution), feedback into rules and skills, and a durable memory substrate are the same structural commitments as Wiener, Ashby, and Beer made explicit for machines and organizations.

## Possible outline

1. **Steering**: *Kybernetes*—why “governor” and “steersman” name the problem (read, reason, act, observe).
2. **Feedback and self-regulation**: `/learn`, behavioral self-adaptation, retrospective instruction improvement—circular causality.
3. **Beer’s VSM**: Map System 5/4 → strategy, 3/3* → truth (Neotoma: audit, provenance), 1/2 → execution (scripts, MCP).
4. **Ashby’s law**: MCPs and scripts as variety amplifiers; rules and skills as constraints—only variety absorbs variety.
5. **Homeostasis**: Local-first, autonomous execution within bounds, escalation only for irreversible or high-risk actions.
6. **Second-order cybernetics**: The system observes itself (constraints, `/learn`, validation)—observer included in the loop.
7. **Neotoma as nervous system**: Without fidelity in observation and memory, feedback loops degrade; entity resolution and event sourcing as signal integrity over time.
8. **Closing**: What this framing buys—design vocabulary (where variety is unmatched), diagnostics (sense vs. model vs. act vs. learn), positioning for anyone who already knows Beer or Wiener.

## Notes to develop

- Wiener (1948) → organizational cybernetics (Beer) → personal/agentic infrastructure as the same pattern at a new scale.
- Tie to existing posts on truth layer and agent memory where it strengthens without repeating.
- Keep claims falsifiable: this is a lens, not a proof that Neotoma is “the” cybernetic stack.

## Raw material (from chat)

The core parallel is control and communication: agents that read structured memory, reason from strategy, execute actions, and adjust behavior from outcomes are cybernetic systems by definition.

**Feedback**: Interventions → durable rules/skills/hooks is textbook corrective feedback.

**VSM mapping**:

| Beer (VSM) | Layer | Role |
|------------|--------|------|
| 5 / 4 | Strategy | Identity, intelligence, principles |
| 3 / 3* | Truth | Coordination, audit—Neotoma as monitoring substrate |
| 1 / 2 | Execution | Operations, dampening oscillation—scripts, workers, MCP |

**Ashby**: Expand repertoire (MCPs, scripts, skills) to match environmental variety; use rules to attenuate where needed.

**Homeostasis**: Self-regulation inside bounds; escalate when perturbation exceeds autonomous capacity.

**Second-order**: System modifies its own controlling—retrospective improvement, agent constraints, validation loops.

**Neotoma**: Information channels need fidelity; without reliable memory, you cannot correct what you cannot observe or remember.

**Utility of the frame**: Variety gaps for roadmap; failure taxonomy (sensing vs. modeling vs. actuation vs. feedback); one-line pitch for readers who know Beer/Wiener—truth layer as the information substrate for a viable personal or organizational agent.

title: What happens when you use agent memory to track your finances
excerpt: I spent a day building a financial dashboard on top of Neotoma and discovered that the real test of a memory system is whether it can hold the mess of real financial data without pretending the mess is clean.
body:
I track my finances through Neotoma. Not a spreadsheet, not Mint, not a dedicated fintech app. The same local-first, agent-driven memory system I built for AI agent state now holds my bank accounts, brokerage positions, crypto custody wallets, tax filings, recurring expenses, loans, and transactions.

Today I spent a full day pushing that setup hard: building out a React dashboard that queries the Neotoma REST API directly, modeling Spanish tax filings as first-class entities, debugging why account values showed up blank, standardizing how monetary amounts get stored across dozens of account types, and arguing with myself about whether agents should be allowed to remove schema properties.

Here's what I found.

## Why financial data

Financial data is a stress test for any data system. It's multi-currency, multi-jurisdiction, multi-institution, and changes over time in ways that matter legally. A bank account has a balance today that differs from last month's balance, and you might need both for tax purposes. A Spanish Modelo 720 filing requires year-end values in EUR for accounts held abroad in USD. A crypto custody wallet is really a collection of on-chain addresses, each with its own balance history, rolled up to a parent entity.

This is not the kind of data you can stuff into a flat table and call it done.

What I wanted was a system where every financial entity has a stable identity, every change is recorded as a dated observation, entities are connected through typed relationships, and an agent can query, update, and reason about the whole graph without me writing bespoke logic for each institution. Neotoma was designed for exactly this, but financial data is where the design meets reality.

## What worked well

### Typed entities with flexible schemas

Each financial account is a `financial_account` entity with a `registry_id` that stays stable across imports. Schwab checking, Fidelity LYFT shares, American Express HYSA buckets, Capital One credit card, twenty-plus crypto custody wallets. Same entity type, different properties depending on the institution. Neotoma's progressive schema system meant I could add `filing_tags`, `strategy_bucket`, `account_value`, and `account_value_currency` to the type over time without breaking the entities that were already stored.

Today I added `tax_filing` as a new entity type to represent actual filings (Modelo 720, Modelo 721, FBAR, US federal) as first-class objects with their own properties: `form_code`, `jurisdiction`, `tax_year`, `status`, `due_date`. Each filing links to its included accounts through relationships. Before this, filings were implicit: the dashboard filtered accounts by `filing_tags` and rendered a view. Now the filing itself is an entity you can track, annotate, and relate to supporting documents.

### Observation history as audit trail

When I store a Schwab brokerage statement for December 31, 2025, that becomes an observation on the `financial_account` entity. The entity's snapshot updates to reflect the latest values, but every previous observation is preserved. If the gestor asks what the balance was on a different date, or if I need to reconcile against a previous import, the history is there. No overwrites.

This came up concretely today when comparing 2024 and 2025 Modelo 720 data. The 2024 workbook had specific EUR values for each account. The 2025 values needed to come from new December 31 statements with fresh FX conversions. Both years of data coexist as observations on the same account entities.

### Relationships that connect the graph

A `tax_filing` (Modelo 720, 2025) relates to twelve `financial_account` entities through `REFERS_TO` relationships. Each account can relate to `account_statement` entities that hold the dated evidence. A `loan` relates to the `financial_account` it's secured against. A crypto `custody_*` account relates to `crypto_wallet_address` entities for its on-chain positions.

When the dashboard loads a filing detail page, it fetches the filing entity, its relationships, and the related entities in a few API calls. The page can show the filing metadata, the included accounts with their values, and the supporting evidence chain. All from the same entity graph.

### Agent-driven data collection

Most of the data that entered Neotoma today was stored by agents, not by me manually. I told an agent to populate `tax_filing` records for 2024 and 2025. It queried existing account entities, looked up the filing requirements, created the filing entities with the right properties, and wired the relationships. When I provided PDF statements (Schwab brokerage, Amex HYSA, Capital One, Fidelity), agents extracted the relevant balances, computed EUR equivalents using the Frankfurter ECB API, and stored the results as observations with proper `as_of_date` values and idempotency keys.

For the Modelo 721 (crypto), an agent cross-referenced the Assets spreadsheet with Neotoma custody entities, resolved which Bitcoin addresses belonged to which custody wallets, pulled December 31 on-chain balances from block explorer APIs, and stored everything with provenance. A separate conversation handled Coinbase positions from a December statement PDF and confirmed that Binance and OKX were closed accounts with zero balances.

The pipeline is not fully automated. I still download PDFs from broker websites in Safari (agents can't log into Schwab). But once the source material exists, agents handle the extraction, conversion, storage, and relationship wiring. The ratio of my time to theirs is getting small.

### Live dashboard on the API

The dashboard itself is a React app that queries Neotoma's REST API: `POST /entities/query` for filtered lists, `GET /entities/:id` for detail, `GET /entities/:id/observations` for history, `GET /entities/:id/relationships` for the graph. It renders accounts, filings, loans, recurring expenses, transactions, a timeline, and a generic entity explorer. Currency conversion happens client-side using the Frankfurter API with local caching.

The fact that I can build a reasonably complete financial dashboard on top of a general-purpose memory API, without any finance-specific server code, says something about the entity-relationship model. The API doesn't know what a `financial_account` is. It stores entities, observations, and relationships. The financial semantics live in the entity types, their schemas, and the dashboard's rendering logic.

## What broke, and what it taught me

### Monetary field inconsistency

The single hardest problem today was not a bug in Neotoma. It was the accumulated inconsistency in how different import sources named monetary fields. Some accounts stored `balance_eur` and `balance_usd`. Others stored `account_value` and `account_value_currency`. Legacy imports had `ending_account_value_usd`. Statements used `market_value_usd`. The dashboard had to check a cascade of field names to find the right number, and sometimes picked the wrong one: an account showing $1,010 as its "converted value" when the raw value was already in dollars.

The fix was to standardize on `account_value` plus `account_value_currency` as the canonical pair, with fallbacks for legacy fields. But the deeper lesson is that flexible schemas are a double-edged sword. The ability to add any property to an entity means agents and import scripts will name things differently unless there's a strong convention enforced at the instruction level, not just the schema level.

### Overloaded entity types

The `financial_account` type had accumulated statement-specific fields: `statement_pdf_path`, `statement_as_of_date`, `statement_period_end`, `ending_account_value_usd`. These belong on a separate `account_statement` entity, linked to the account through a relationship. Having them on the account itself meant the entity grew cluttered over time, with dated evidence mixed into the durable identity.

Today I started breaking statements out into their own entities. It's the right modeling decision, but it's also a migration: existing accounts have these fields baked in, and the dashboard has to handle both shapes during the transition. In a traditional database you'd run an ALTER TABLE and a backfill script. In Neotoma, the old observations with the old shape are still there, and the new observations use the new shape. The snapshot reflects the latest, which is what you want, but it means the entity's properties include both the legacy and current fields until the old ones are explicitly deprecated.

### The schema property removal question

This led to a genuinely hard design question. Neotoma lets agents add properties to a schema but not remove them. The rationale is determinism: schema evolution should be additive so that replaying observations always produces the same result. But in practice, agents don't know upfront what properties an entity type will need. They experiment, store data with field names that turn out to be wrong, and the schema accumulates cruft.

I spent a conversation today debating whether property removal should be supported. The tension is real. Without removal, entities end up with noisy, outdated properties that confuse both agents and humans reading the snapshot. With removal, you introduce a class of schema change that's harder to reason about during replay. My current position is that removal should be supported, with the old schema version preserved so that prior observations can still be interpreted in their original context. The system stays deterministic in the context of evolving schemas, not frozen ones.

### Filing pages that 404'd

When I first added `tax_filing` entities and tried to navigate to their detail pages, the dashboard returned 404. The filing page route existed but expected a different URL pattern than what the filings index was generating. This was a straightforward React Router bug, but it surfaced because the data model changed: filings went from being implicit views (filter accounts by tag) to explicit entities (navigate to `/filings/ent_xyz`). The UI layer had to catch up to the data model change, and for a few conversations, agents were debugging routing mismatches rather than financial data.

### Timeline gaps

The timeline page in the dashboard fetches from `GET /timeline`. It can return empty even when the database is full of entities and observations, because timeline events are a separate materialized index. Financial activity creates observations on entities, but those observations don't automatically appear in the timeline unless the timeline indexer covers them. This is a known gap. The mental model ("everything that happened should show up on the timeline") doesn't match the implementation ("only events that were explicitly indexed show up").

## What I'd build differently if starting over

Not much, honestly. The entity-relationship model is right for this domain. Typed entities with stable IDs, immutable observations, and explicit relationships capture the structure of financial data naturally. A bank account is an entity. A statement is an observation (or a related entity). A filing is an entity that relates to accounts. Currency conversion is a computation over entity properties, not stored state.

The things I'd do differently are all at the convention layer:

- Establish canonical field names per entity type from day one, documented in the schema itself, not just in markdown docs agents might not read.
- Break out `account_statement` as a first-class type from the start, rather than overloading `financial_account`.
- Build the schema evolution story (including property deprecation) before entities accumulate cruft.
- Wire timeline indexing to cover all observation types by default, with an opt-out rather than opt-in model.

## The bigger picture

Using Neotoma for financial data tracking is not about replacing Mint or building a fintech product. It's about testing whether a general-purpose, agent-driven memory system can handle a domain that demands precision, auditability, and multi-dimensional relationships. Finance is where you find out whether your abstractions actually hold.

So far, they hold. The entity model scales to dozens of account types, the observation history provides the audit trail that tax filings require, and the relationship graph connects the pieces that need connecting. Where the system falls short is not in the core architecture but in the tooling around schema hygiene, field naming conventions, and making the full activity stream visible without manual indexing.

These are solvable problems. And every one of them surfaced because I was using the system for something real, not running synthetic tests. That's what build-in-public means in practice: the hardest bugs are the ones that only appear when the data is messy, the requirements are real, and the deadline is a Spanish tax filing.
summary:
- Financial data stress-tests a memory system because it's multi-currency, multi-jurisdiction, and changes over time in legally meaningful ways.
- Neotoma's typed entities, observation history, and relationship graph naturally model bank accounts, tax filings, crypto custody wallets, and the connections between them.
- Agent-driven data collection handled statement extraction, FX conversion, entity creation, and relationship wiring with minimal manual intervention.
- The hardest problem was not architecture but convention: inconsistent field names across import sources forced the dashboard into a cascade of fallback checks.
- Schema property removal is a real design tension. Additive-only schemas preserve determinism but accumulate cruft that confuses agents and humans.
- Entity types get overloaded when dated evidence (statements) lives on durable entities (accounts) instead of on related entities with their own lifecycle.
- The things that break in production are the ones that only surface with real, messy data and real deadlines.

title: Tweet
body:
## Tweet

Pre-PMF, your agents need to remember so you can iterate faster. Post-PMF, they need to be accountable because users depend on them. Same infrastructure, different pitch.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/agent-memory-before-and-after-product-market-fit

## LinkedIn

There's a popular take right now: Claude Code prototypes aren't production systems. Add testing, validation, observability. That's the work that actually matters.

True. But only once you've found product-market fit.

Before PMF, the work that matters is learning. Every hour on governance for a product nobody uses is an hour not spent finding out whether anyone cares. The teams I've seen waste the most time aren't the ones who shipped too early. They're the ones who hardened something nobody asked for.

I'm building Neotoma as a deterministic state layer for long-running agents. The same architecture serves both phases. Pre-PMF founders get reliable agent memory so they stop wasting time re-explaining context. Post-PMF teams get inspectable, replayable state so they can answer "what happened?" when an agent makes a bad decision.

Same system. Two different entry points.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/agent-memory-before-and-after-product-market-fit

title: There's a take circulating in the Claude Code discourse that goes something like
body:
There's a take circulating in the Claude Code discourse that goes something like this: prototypes built in hours don't have race condition handling, audit trails, or recovery paths. The governance layer is the work that actually matters.

I agree with the diagnosis. But "the work that actually matters" depends entirely on where you are.

## The phase determines the work

Before product-market fit, the work that matters is finding out whether anyone wants what you're building. Every hour spent on governance for a product nobody uses is an hour not spent learning. Hardening something nobody asked for is the most expensive kind of waste.

After product-market fit, the equation flips. Users depend on your system. Agents act on their behalf. State changes have consequences. Now the governance layer is load-bearing.

The builders I've seen burn the most time aren't the ones who shipped a prototype too early. They're the ones who hardened something nobody asked for. Or, post-PMF, the ones who kept shipping prototypes when their users needed reliability.

The skill isn't knowing how to harden. It's knowing when.

## What this means for agent memory

I'm building [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) as a deterministic state layer for long-running agents. The value it provides maps differently depending on what stage the user is at.

Pre-PMF, the people I'm building for look like this:

**Solo founders and small teams running agents across sessions.** They're using Cursor, Claude Code, or multi-tool MCP setups to move fast. They open a session, ask an agent to pick up where yesterday left off, and the agent either remembers or it doesn't.

When it doesn't, they waste time re-explaining context. When it does remember but gets something wrong, they waste time debugging invisible state. When they can't tell what the agent decided or why, they stop trusting it and start doing the work manually again.

For these users, Neotoma's value is speed. Agents that carry reliable context across sessions mean fewer restarts, less re-explanation, and more time spent on the actual problem: does anyone want this thing?

They don't need audit trails. They don't need compliance. They need their agents to work consistently so they can learn faster.

## Post-PMF, the problem changes shape

The users here look different. Product teams with agents in production workflows. Developer integrators embedding agent-driven features in shipped software. AI tool builders whose customers depend on agent behavior being predictable.

When an agent double-charges a customer, the first question is: what happened? When a workflow produces a wrong result, the question is: what did the agent decide, and can we replay it? When state drifts over weeks of accumulated context, the question is: how did we get here?

For these users, Neotoma's value is trust. Versioned state evolution. Replayable transitions. Audit trails that actually answer the question. Not because it's nice to have, but because their users require it.

## Same infrastructure, different entry points

The underlying architecture doesn't change between these two use cases. Event-sourced observations. Entity resolution. Typed relationships. Schema-bound state. These properties serve both speed and trust, just weighted differently.

A pre-PMF founder using Neotoma gets reliable cross-session memory for their agents. Entities and relationships persist. Context doesn't drift. The agent picks up where it left off because the state layer underneath is deterministic, not because it "feels like" it remembers.

A post-PMF team using the same infrastructure gets something else on top: the ability to inspect, diff, replay, and audit what their agents did. The event log that was quietly accumulating during the scrappy early phase becomes the audit trail the compliance team needs later.

This is the part most tools get wrong. They optimize for one phase or the other. Convenience-first systems work for speed but collapse under scrutiny. Enterprise governance tools work for compliance but add too much friction to be useful pre-PMF.

## The positioning trap

I think about this tension often. Neotoma's developer release targets builders with real agent state problems. When I describe it as "deterministic state integrity for long-running agents," that resonates with post-PMF teams who have been burned. But it can sound like overhead to a pre-PMF founder who just wants their agents to stop forgetting things.

The same system. Two different pitches.

"Your agents remember reliably" for the founder iterating at speed.

"Your agents are accountable" for the team that ships to customers.

Both are true. The question is which one you lead with, and the answer depends on who you're talking to.

## Why I care about getting this right

I'm pre-PMF myself. I'm building Neotoma and using it every day in my own agentic stack: 12+ MCP servers, persistent rules, reusable skills, structured memory underneath. I'm the Tier 1 user. The solo founder who needs agents to carry context reliably so I can focus on learning whether this thing matters.

But I'm building the architecture as though the post-PMF use case is coming. Event sourcing isn't a premature optimization when the alternative is state you can't inspect. Schema constraints aren't overhead when the alternative is memory that silently contradicts itself.

The foundation has to be right from the start. The pitch can change depending on who walks through the door.
summary:
- Pre-PMF, the work that matters is learning whether anyone wants what you're building. Hardening a product nobody uses is the most expensive kind of waste.
- For early-stage founders running agents across sessions, Neotoma's value is speed: reliable context that sticks so they can iterate instead of re-explaining.
- Post-PMF teams need accountability. When agents act on behalf of users, state has to be inspectable, auditable, and replayable.
- The same event-sourced architecture serves both phases. Cross-session memory for speed becomes an audit trail for trust as the product matures.
- The positioning trap: "deterministic state integrity" resonates post-PMF but sounds like overhead pre-PMF. Same system, two different entry points.

title: The AI debate is replaying one of the oldest arguments in modern thought. Rousse
body:
The AI debate is replaying one of the oldest arguments in modern thought. Rousseau said tools corrupt us. Condorcet said they advance us. Hobbes said to constrain them. And Kant said the real question is whether people choose to engage with them. They all got part of it right.

Nietzsche got past the ceiling the others hit. He didn't ask whether the tool is good or bad; he asked what you become in the encounter with it. The person who avoids AI to protect a skill is preserving, not growing. The person who surrenders to AI and stops developing is declining. Both paths lead to a smaller self.

The Nietzschean position is harder than either side of the current debate. The technology is here, and it will change what it means to think, create, and work. That change is not a threat to run from nor a gift to passively receive. It is material, and what you make of it is the only answer that matters.

Every major technology has forced this same question. The costs were never resolved by the people who stayed away. They were resolved by the people who engaged, and who were inevitably changed by the process.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-the-technology-asks-of-you

title: Tweet
body:
## Tweet

Rousseau said tools corrupt us. Condorcet said they advance us. Hobbes said to control them. Kant said to choose to engage with them.

The AI debate is replaying all four. Nietzsche asked the question they missed: what do you become?

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-the-technology-asks-of-you

## LinkedIn

The AI debate is replaying one of the oldest arguments in modern thought. Rousseau said tools corrupt us. Condorcet said they advance us. Hobbes said to constrain them. And Kant said the real question is whether people choose to engage with them. They all got part of it right.

Nietzsche got past the ceiling the others hit. He didn't ask whether the tool is good or bad; he asked what you become in the encounter with it. The person who avoids AI to protect a skill is preserving, not growing. The person who surrenders to AI and stops developing is declining. Both paths lead to a smaller self.

The Nietzschean position is harder than either side of the current debate. The technology is here, and it will change what it means to think, create, and work. That change is not a threat to run from nor a gift to passively receive. It is material, and what you make of it is the only answer that matters.

Every major technology has forced this same question. The costs were never resolved by the people who stayed away. They were resolved by the people who engaged, and who were inevitably changed by the process.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-the-technology-asks-of-you

title: Tweet
body:
## Tweet

Anti-guiri populism in Spain and America First nationalism are the same move. Both blame an out-group defined by origin for structural problems. The evidence doesn't support it. Severity differs; the logic doesn't.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/scapegoating-foreigners-bipartisan-international

## LinkedIn

The "tourists go home" protests in Spain and MAGA-style immigration restriction in the US use the same playbook. Both channel real material grievances toward an out-group defined by national origin, then restrict their economic participation.

The evidence doesn't support the blame in either case. In Spain, foreigners contribute 10% of Social Security revenue while drawing 1% of spending, and tourism generates 16% of GDP. The power asymmetry runs the opposite way from what many assume: locals vote, expats don't.

Severity matters. Being deported is worse than facing a rent cap. But the underlying logic is the same, and it escalates once accepted. I wrote about why "punching up" isn't philosophically defensible, and why even interventionist policy solutions beat scapegoating.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/scapegoating-foreigners-bipartisan-international

The evidence doesn't support the blame in either case. In Spain, foreigners contribute 10% of Social Security revenue while drawing 1% of spending, and tourism generates 16% of GDP. The power asymmetry runs the opposite way from what many assume: locals vote, expats don't.

Severity matters. Being deported is worse than facing a rent cap. But the underlying logic is the same, and it escalates once accepted. I wrote about why "punching up" isn't philosophically defensible, and why even interventionist policy solutions beat scapegoating.

title: Scapegoating foreigners runs the political spectrum and knows no borders
excerpt: Anti-guiri populism in Spain and America First nationalism use the same playbook. Both channel real grievances toward an out-group defined by origin, then restrict their economic participation. The evidence does not support the blame.
body:
There's a protest meme that's been circulating in Barcelona for a few years now: *Tourists go home.* I've seen it on my own street, sprayed on walls in the Gòtic, chanted at demonstrations in the Barceloneta, printed on stickers slapped across Airbnb lockboxes. In the summer of 2024, [the meme went physical](https://www.cnn.com/): thousands of protesters marched through the Barceloneta spraying tourists with water guns, driving bewildered diners from restaurant patios mid-meal.[^1] The images went global. [Repeat demonstrations followed in 2025](https://www.npr.org/), coordinated across Barcelona, Mallorca, and other Spanish cities.[^2]

The water gun protest was widely covered as quirky, even charming. But deliberately spraying strangers is, under Spanish law, a form of *maltrato de obra* (minor physical mistreatment).[^3] That we instinctively read it as harmless says something about which groups we consider acceptable targets.

The sentiment is familiar even if you've never set foot in Catalonia. Swap "tourists" for "immigrants," swap Barcelona for any mid-sized American city, and you'd have a MAGA rally.

That comparison makes people uncomfortable on both sides. The Barcelona left doesn't want to be compared to American nativism. The American right doesn't want to be lumped in with European progressives. The magnitude differs, the policy tools differ, but the structural logic is identical. The main thing that actually varies is the socioeconomic direction of the scapegoating, and that distinction doesn't survive scrutiny.

## The same playbook

Both movements channel genuine material grievances (housing affordability in Barcelona, wage stagnation in the US) and direct them at a visible "other" defined by national origin. In Barcelona, that's the [*guiri*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guiri): the foreign tourist, the digital nomad, the Northern European expat buying a flat in Gràcia. In the US, it's the immigrant: undocumented workers, asylum seekers, anyone who arrived from somewhere poorer.

The emotional logic is the same in both cases. *Our place is being taken from us by people who don't belong here.* And in both cases, the proposed solution follows the same template: use collective political power to restrict the out-group's access to housing, work, or public space.

Barcelona's policy toolkit includes banning all short-term tourist rentals by 2028,[^4] proposing restrictions on non-EU foreign property purchases,[^5] and implementing strict rent controls that disproportionately affect foreign landlords.[^20] America's toolkit includes border walls, deportation campaigns, and work authorization restrictions. Different targets, same mechanism: identify a class of people by where they were born, then curtail their economic participation.

The comparison doesn't only run between Spain's left and America's right. Both countries have both versions.

Spain has Vox, its own right-wing anti-immigration movement, which channels resentment toward lower-income immigrants from North Africa and Latin America using rhetoric interchangeable with an American nativist playbook.

America has its own version of targeting wealthy foreign buyers: Florida passed a law in 2023 banning certain Chinese nationals from purchasing property in the state, drawing comparisons to the xenophobic [alien land laws](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Alien_land_laws) of the early twentieth century.[^7] Critics within the Asian American community called it racist; supporters framed it as protecting local housing affordability and national security. Barcelona's proposed ban on non-EU foreign property purchases could have been drafted from the same template.

The political spectrum doesn't determine which outsiders get blamed. It determines which *direction* the blame flows. The left scapegoats upward; the right scapegoats downward. Both are scapegoating.

## The causal story is equally weak in both cases

If you're going to restrict a group's freedoms, you'd better have strong evidence that they're actually causing the problem you're trying to solve. In neither case does the evidence support the weight being placed on it.

In Barcelona, foreign buyers account for roughly 25–30% of property purchases[^8], a significant share. But the decade-long trend in total Spanish property transactions has been strongly upward, nearly doubling since 2013, driven primarily by domestic buyers who make up the vast majority of the market. The foreign share spiked to record levels in late 2023 not because of a surge in foreign buying but because ECB rate hikes temporarily crushed local mortgage-dependent demand: local buyers fell 25% year-over-year in Q4 2023 while foreign buyers (who more often purchase without Spanish mortgages) held steady.[^9] The headline "record foreign market share" was largely a denominator effect from a cyclical correction, not evidence of foreigners taking over the market.

Peer-reviewed econometric research on Airbnb's impact found that short-term rentals raised rents by about 1.9% citywide on average, and by up to 7% in the most tourist-heavy neighborhoods, with transaction prices rising 4.6% citywide and up to 17% in high-activity areas.[^10] Those neighborhood-level effects are real and painful. But they aren't doubling or tripling prices as protest rhetoric implies. And to the extent that short-term rental regulation targets the *behavior* of converting housing to tourist use, it's legitimate policy by any standard: a Catalan investor running the same Airbnb arbitrage does identical damage, and a ban that applies to all operators regardless of nationality is regulation, not scapegoating. Barcelona had no comprehensive tourist accommodation zoning until the [PEUAT](https://ajuntament.barcelona.cat/pla-allotjaments-turistics/en) was adopted in 2017, and the revised plan didn't take full effect until 2022; Airbnb's explosive growth happened in that regulatory vacuum, with thousands of unlicensed listings operating for years before enforcement caught up.[^24] The problem is when the policy response shifts from targeting the behavior to targeting the person's origin, as with proposed bans on non-EU property purchases. The STR damage was caused by a regulatory failure, not by who held the passport.

Meanwhile, the deeper structural drivers get buried beneath the simpler narrative of *the foreigners did it.* Spain's housing starts have fallen to levels not seen since the early 1970s.[^11] Roughly 75,000 Barcelona homes sit genuinely vacant.[^12] The 30% social housing quota on new developments has paradoxically deterred construction.[^13] The core problem is a structural gap between household formation and construction output, combined with a generational shift from ownership to renting and internal migration concentrating demand in the cities where supply is most constrained, dynamics overwhelmingly driven by Spanish nationals, not foreigners.[^14][^15][^16]

The American case is a mirror image. The economics literature on immigration and wages is genuinely contested, with estimates ranging from slightly positive to a 6-7% reduction for native low-skill workers.[^17][^18] But the most important drivers of wage stagnation, technological change, offshoring, the decline of unions, financialization, are structural, not demographic. Undocumented workers contribute roughly $90 billion annually in taxes and hold $299 billion in spending power.[^19]

A fair analysis requires distinguishing between the very different populations that *guiri* lumps together. A tourist on a three-day trip contributes spending and leaves. A digital nomad earning a foreign salary and renting an Airbnb competes directly with locals for housing without contributing to local labor markets or Social Security. A long-term expat on a Spanish employment contract pays into Social Security and, if they hold EU citizenship, can vote in municipal elections. The Social Security data applies to foreign workers, not tourists. The Airbnb research applies to short-term rental operators, not long-term residents. Pulling these apart actually strengthens the case against origin-based restrictions, because the populations doing the most measurable harm (speculative short-term rental operators) are not defined by nationality, while the populations defined by nationality (foreign workers, long-term residents) are net fiscal contributors.

In both cases, the scapegoated group is, on net, subsidizing the locals more than they're burdening them. In Spain the numbers are stark: international visitors spent over €134 billion in 2025; the broader tourism sector contributed roughly €260 billion, about 16% of GDP.[^21] Foreign workers contributed about 10% of total Social Security revenue while accounting for only about 1% of its expenditure, driven largely by the immigrant workforce's younger age profile.[^22] The fiscal transfer runs decisively from foreigners to locals, not the other way around.

And in both cases, group-neutral fixes exist: remove the zoning and permitting barriers that prevent housing from being built, open legal labor markets so workers aren't trapped in black-market exploitation, enforce contracts and property rights universally. None of these require identifying a villain by national origin. They require getting bad rules out of the way.

## The power asymmetry runs the other way

Here's where the conventional progressive framing breaks down completely.

The standard defense of anti-guiri politics goes something like this: *It's different because the foreigners in Spain are rich. They have more power. Restricting them isn't like restricting poor immigrants, it's punching up.* This reasoning looks at individual economic power and concludes that a wealthy expat facing regulatory friction is categorically different from a poor migrant facing deportation.

But this conflates individual economic power with collective political power, and when you look at the political dimension, the asymmetry reverses.

A Spanish citizen in Barcelona, even a struggling one, can vote in municipal, regional, and national elections. They can join a tenants' union with real political leverage. They can run for office. They participate in the regulatory process that determines whether an expat can rent, buy, work, or stay.

Most expats have little or none of that. EU citizens resident in Spain can vote in and stand for municipal elections, a meaningful form of participation. But non-EU residents cannot vote at any level, and no foreigner votes in regional or national elections, where housing and immigration policy are actually set. All expats are subject to rules they had no part in shaping at the levels that matter most, and when those rules change, as they do frequently in Barcelona's volatile housing policy environment, they have no recourse except compliance or departure.

This is structurally identical to the situation of immigrants in the US. American citizens, even economically marginal ones, have the franchise. They have representatives. They have collective bargaining power through political institutions. The undocumented immigrant has none of that.

I have skin in this game. I'm an American expat earning a Silicon Valley salary while paying Barcelona rents, which makes me exactly the kind of person this essay defends. That doesn't make the argument wrong, but it means I owe it more rigor, not less, and the reader should weigh it accordingly.

It's also worth questioning just how vast the economic power gap between expats and locals really is. Some fit my profile.

But many expats who come to Barcelona work to integrate their professional lives locally, earning local wages and dealing with the same questions of economic viability that their Spanish neighbors face. The gap between a Spanish software developer and a French one working at the same Barcelona startup is not the gap between a hedge fund manager and a factory worker. The "rich foreigner" framing flattens a diverse population into a single, convenient caricature.

There's an even more telling wrinkle: some of the loudest anti-guiri voices are guiris themselves. Expats who arrived years ago now complain about newer arrivals driving up rents and changing the character of their adopted neighborhoods. This is the "pulling up the ladder" phenomenon, and it has an exact American parallel, where immigrants who gained legal status advocate for stricter restrictions on those who came after them. In both cases, people who benefited from freedom of movement turn around and argue that others shouldn't have the same access. The scapegoating logic is so powerful it captures even the scapegoated group.

The implicit reasoning behind "expats just face friction, not oppression" is really just: *they have somewhere else to go and enough money to get there.* But that's the same argument restrictionists make about immigrants. *You chose to come here; you can choose to leave if you don't like the terms.* And it's worth noting that locals also have an exit option. The practical costs are asymmetric: uprooting from a city where your family, language, professional network, and community ties are rooted is harder than an expat returning to a country where those ties already exist. But the asymmetry of exit costs doesn't justify restricting the economic participation of one group based on where they were born. Both groups choose to stay. Neither is literally trapped.

Progressive politics rejects this argument when it's aimed downward on the economic ladder, recognizing that it treats freedom of movement as a privilege rather than a right. But it accepts the identical argument when aimed upward, because it *feels* different to restrict someone richer than you versus someone poorer.

The feeling is understandable. It's not an ethical argument.

## Culture, severity, and the ratchet

There's a version of this argument that isn't about economics at all: *the foreigners are displacing our culture.* Catalan and Spanish are being crowded out by English in commercial districts. Neighborhood bars become brunch spots. The texture of the place changes.

I take this seriously as a description. But what's being described is multiculturalism. Cultures have always mixed fluidly. The results are never "even" in the sense that every tradition survives in equal, static proportions. Some forms recede. New ones emerge. It's fair to acknowledge that market-driven cultural change can feel coercive to people with less purchasing power. When a neighborhood shifts to serve wealthier newcomers' preferences, English menus replacing Catalan ones, coworking spaces replacing neighborhood bars, locals who can't afford the new price level are effectively priced out of their own cultural landscape. That's a real loss, and dismissing it as progress would be glib. But the answer is to address the underlying market dynamics (housing supply, commercial rent policy, public space protections) rather than restricting who can show up. The desire to preserve cultural "purity" through origin-based restrictions is the same impulse whether it comes from a Catalan nationalist worried about English signage or an American nativist worried about Spanish being spoken in a Texas grocery store.

Does severity matter? Of course. Being deported is worse than facing a rent cap. A property purchase ban is not the same as detention. But the underlying logic is the same, and history shows that the severity tends to escalate once the logic is accepted. The escalation did not happen in a straight line. Trump's first term (2017-2021) began with rhetoric about "bad hombres," moved to a travel ban, and escalated to family separations at the border. The Biden administration reversed some of these policies, but the Overton window had shifted. Trump's second term picked up where the first left off, with mass deportation operations and attempts to revoke birthright citizenship. The pattern is not continuous escalation but a ratchet that resets higher after each cycle. Barcelona's policy arc, from protest slogans to rental bans to proposed foreign purchase restrictions, follows the same ratchet at a lower intensity. Accepting the principle that origin-based restrictions are legitimate, even mild ones, creates the precedent for harsher ones later.

## A darker precedent

The idea that scapegoating wealthier outsiders is somehow safer or more justified than scapegoating poorer ones has a grim historical track record. The most catastrophic example is antisemitism, which for centuries operated and continues to operate precisely on the logic that targeting an economically successful minority was "punching up" — that Jewish commercial success was itself the problem, that it came at the expense of the local population, and that restricting Jewish economic participation was a form of self-defense for the native majority.

The Rwandan genocide followed a strikingly similar structure. The Tutsi minority had historically occupied the higher economic and social strata, a hierarchy Belgian colonists hardened into rigid ethnic categories through mandatory identity cards. When the Hutu majority gained political power, the Tutsi were framed simultaneously as wealthy oppressors and as foreigners — not truly indigenous to Rwanda. Hutu Power propaganda designated them "oppressors of the Hutu" because of their perceived economic dominance, and cast their removal as liberation. The result was the murder of over 800,000 people in a hundred days.

I'm not predicting that anti-guiri sentiment in Barcelona will escalate to anything resembling antisemitism or the Rwandan genocide. The point is philosophical, not predictive. "Punching up" is often treated as inherently defensible. These are the starkest illustrations of why it isn't. The structural logic, *this group's relative wealth is the cause of our hardship, and restricting their economic participation is the solution*, is the same logic. When someone says "it's different because they're rich," they're making the exact argument that has historically justified targeting economically successful minorities. The direction of the economic gradient does not determine whether the move is just. Wealth-based discrimination is still discrimination. What determines whether a restriction is justified is not whether it targets people richer or poorer than you, but whether you're attributing systemic problems to a group defined by identity rather than addressing the systems themselves.

## This isn't a denial of power structures

The predictable objection to everything above is: *you're just a privileged person denying that power structures exist.* So let me be clear about where this argument comes from.

I've spent a long time thinking about power, starting with [an honors thesis on Nietzsche's theory of morality](/honors-thesis), which is essentially an extended study of how power operates through subtle forms of morality itself. Nietzsche's central insight in [*On the Genealogy of Morals*](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/On_the_Genealogy_of_Morality) is that power isn't exercised only by the dominant. The priestly class, physically defeated by the aristocratic class, achieves a "radical revaluation of their enemies' values," redefining strength as evil and weakness as virtue. Power flows in both directions: the strong exert it through force, the weak exert it through moral frameworks that recast submission as righteousness and dominance as sin.

This matters here because both movements are, in Nietzschean terms, forms of [ressentiment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Resentment#Ressentiment) that have successfully moralized themselves. The anti-guiri claim isn't "we want to restrict foreigners' economic participation because it benefits us politically." It's "restricting foreigners is *justice*, it's punching up, it's decolonial, it's protecting the vulnerable." The MAGA version runs the same play in a different register: a movement of people who believe themselves disempowered by elites and outsiders reframes "they're taking our jobs" as "we're defending American workers," and immigration restriction becomes patriotism rather than exclusion. In both cases, the moral framing is the power move. And it's effective precisely because it's invisible as such.

The definition of who is disempowered rarely maps cleanly onto identity categories. A Barcelona local priced out of their neighborhood is disempowered. So is an expat subject to laws they have no political voice to shape. So is an undocumented worker in the US with no recourse against wage theft. The question isn't *which group deserves empowerment*, it's whether your framework for empowerment requires disempowering someone else defined by where they were born.

Recognizing that power structures are real, complex, and multidirectional is precisely what leads me to reject the binary framing that either the locals or the foreigners must win. Both can be empowered by better institutions and technologies. Neither needs to be scapegoated for the other to thrive.

## The false choice

There's a move that both left and right populism make here that needs to be named: framing this as a competition between the interests of locals and outsiders, as if addressing one group's concerns requires sacrificing the other's.

This is a false choice.

It's worth acknowledging where the anger is correctly aimed, even if it's misdirected at the wrong target. Spain's [golden visa](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immigrant_investor_programs) program, which from 2013 to 2025 granted residency to non-EU nationals who invested €500,000 or more in real estate, concentrated thousands of investment purchases in Barcelona, Madrid, and Málaga.[^23] The policy itself wasn't wrong: attracting capital and talent through open investment rules is exactly the kind of institution-level approach this essay advocates. The failure was pairing it with the same restrictive zoning, inadequate construction, and regulatory bottlenecks that constrain housing supply for everyone. Had Spain simultaneously deregulated its real estate development pipeline to let supply respond, golden visa investment would have expanded the housing stock rather than competing for a fixed one. Blaming the investors who responded to the incentive, rather than the governments that created the bottleneck, is a textbook case of scapegoating.

Some housing economists argue that non-resident speculation is a distinct market failure warranting targeted policy, as Canada and New Zealand have done. But the test for whether a policy is a market correction or a form of scapegoating is simple: does it target the behavior or the person's origin? A vacancy tax hits anyone who leaves a property empty, local or foreign. A ban on non-EU purchases hits only people born in the wrong place. The first is regulation. The second is discrimination with an economics paper attached.

This test applies to the economic participation of people already living in a community. Entry policy is a different question: visa regimes and immigration quotas inherently use origin as a criterion, and reasonable people can disagree about the right levels.[^25] The core claim holds for the populations both movements primarily target: people already participating in the local economy.

Housing scarcity and labor exploitation are not caused by the wrong people showing up. They are caused by rules that prevent supply from responding to demand: [zoning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Zoning_in_the_United_States) that caps density, permitting regimes that take years, [rent controls](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rent_regulation) that shrink the long-term rental stock, [occupational licensing](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Occupational_licensing) that locks workers out of legal employment. Remove the barriers and let people build, work, and trade freely. My own preference is for these deregulatory fixes. But even if you favor more interventionist solutions (public housing construction, inclusionary zoning mandates, rent stabilization), those are still better than scapegoating because they target the problem, not a group of people defined by where they were born.

The obvious objection is timing: structural reform takes years, and people's rents are rising now. That's fair. But the short-term toolkit doesn't require targeting anyone by nationality either. Vacancy taxes, short-term rental caps, emergency permitting fast-tracks for affordable housing, and enforcement against illegal tourist apartments all address the immediate pressure without sorting people by passport. The urgency of the crisis is real. It does not justify origin-based restrictions when behavior-based tools are available today. The classical liberal insight is that you design institutions around principles rather than around groups, precisely because the moment you start allocating rights and restrictions by group membership, you've created a hierarchy. Even if you believe you're creating it in the right direction.

The anti-guiri activist who wants to restrict foreign property ownership and the MAGA [restrictionist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Immigration_reduction_in_the_United_States) who wants to limit immigration are making the same foundational move: defining a class of people by origin, then using collective political power to curtail that class's access to economic participation. The policy specifics differ. The underlying logic, *people from here deserve preferential access to economic life here*, is the same.

## What scapegoating actually is

The deepest parallel between these movements isn't ideological. It's functional.

Removing the zoning caps, permit bottlenecks, and regulatory barriers that prevent housing from being built is bureaucratically tedious and threatens entrenched interests. Opening legal labor markets means confronting the licensing regimes and work-authorization rules that keep supply artificially scarce. These fixes are slow, boring, and offer no emotional payoff. Blaming guiris or immigrants, by contrast, is viscerally satisfying and electorally useful. It gives voters a face to attach to their frustration and politicians a story simpler than "our institutional failures span decades and implicate all of us."

The scapegoating isn't a means to a policy end. In both cases, it *is* the product. It's what's being sold to voters. And the cost is borne by people who have no political voice to object, which, in a democracy that claims to care about rights, should be precisely the people we're most careful about targeting.

Either people have a legitimate claim to participate in the communities they live in regardless of national origin, or they don't. You can't make that principle conditional on wealth.

---

### Notes

[^1]: [CNN](https://www.cnn.com/), "Barcelona anti-tourism protesters fire water pistols at visitors," July 8, 2024.

[^2]: [NPR](https://www.npr.org/), "Spaniards turn water pistols on visitors to protest tourism," June 16, 2025.

[^3]: [Spanish Penal Code](https://www.boe.es/buscar/act.php?id=BOE-A-1995-25444) (*Ley Orgánica 10/1995*), Article 147.3, covering *maltrato de obra*, striking or mistreating another person without causing injury.

[^4]: [Euronews](https://www.euronews.com/), "Spain's housing crisis deepens: residents' anger is rising," August 6, 2025. Mayor Collboni announced the elimination of all short-term rental licenses by 2028.

[^5]: [Spanish Property Insight](https://www.spanishpropertyinsight.com/), "Barcelona considers banning foreign investors and non-residents from housing market," January 25, 2025.

[^7]: [NBC News](https://www.nbcnews.com/news/asian-america/desantis-chinese-homeownership-ban-florida-rcna120456), "How DeSantis' ban on Chinese homeownership has affected buyers and real estate agents 3 months in," October 18, 2023. Florida's SB 264 banned certain Chinese nationals from purchasing property; critics compared it to xenophobic alien land laws.

[^8]: [Spanish Property Insight](https://www.spanishpropertyinsight.com/), "Barcelona property market reports," January 2026. Foreign buyers reached a decade-high 24% market share in 2024. Investropa estimated approximately 30% by September 2025.

[^9]: [Spanish Property Insight](https://www.spanishpropertyinsight.com/), "Foreign buyers take record share of Barcelona housing market," March 10, 2024. Local buyers fell 25% in Q4 2023 while foreign purchases remained flat.

[^10]: Garcia-López, Jofre-Monseny, Martínez-Mazza, and Segú, [Do short-term rental platforms affect housing markets? Evidence from Airbnb in Barcelona](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0094119019301152), *Journal of Urban Economics*, 2020.

[^11]: OECD/INE housing starts data, available via [FRED](https://fred.stlouisfed.org/series/WSCNDW01ESA470N) (Work Started: Construction: Dwellings and Residential Buildings: Total for Spain). Annual starts fell below 100,000 units in 2023-2024, compared to peaks above 700,000 in 2006.

[^12]: [INE](https://www.ine.es/) 2021 Census, electricity-consumption-based vacancy classification. [El País](https://elpais.com/espana/catalunya/2023-11-02/barcelona-tiene-un-93-de-los-pisos-vacios-segun-una-nueva-estadistica-basada-en-los-consumos-electricos.html), "Barcelona tiene un 9,3% de los pisos vacíos," November 2, 2023. See also [El Nacional](https://www.elnacional.cat/oneconomia/es/economia/cuantos-pisos-vacios-segundas-residencias-hay-en-tu-municipio-en-catalunya-4-cada-10_1117690_102.html) for Catalan breakdown.

[^13]: [Spanish Property Insight](https://www.spanishpropertyinsight.com/), "Barcelona considers banning foreign investors and non-residents from housing market," January 25, 2025.

[^14]: [Statista](https://www.statista.com/topics/7939/residential-rental-market-in-spain/), "Residential rental market in Spain." Nearly a fifth of Spain's population resided in rental properties by 2019, up steadily from prior years.

[^15]: [Brookings Institution](https://www.brookings.edu/), "Spain's once-substantial rental market is now one of the smallest in Europe," April 2021.

[^16]: [CaixaBank Research](https://www.caixabankresearch.com/), "Population and home prices in Spain: a close relationship," March 2024. Spain's population grew by over one million in 2022–2023 (+939,000 foreign nationals, +86,000 Spanish nationals), concentrated in Madrid and Barcelona.

[^17]: Estimates range from Ottaviano and Peri (2012), finding small positive wage effects for all education levels, to Borjas (2003, 2014), estimating wage losses for native high-school dropouts of 3.1–6.3%. See also [Brookings Institution](https://www.brookings.edu/), "What Immigration Means for U.S. Employment and Wages," 2012.

[^18]: Card and Peri, [Immigration Economics by George J. Borjas: A Review Essay](https://pubs.aeaweb.org/doi/10.1257/jel.20161352), *Journal of Economic Literature*, 2016.

[^19]: [Council on Foreign Relations](https://www.cfr.org/), "How Does Immigration Affect the U.S. Economy?" December 4, 2025.

[^20]: Spain's 2023 Housing Law (Ley de Vivienda) caps rent increases and designates "stressed" areas with stricter limits; "large landlord" rules (five or more properties) apply stricter caps. Non-resident and foreign owners are overrepresented among multi-property landlords in Catalonia. See [The Local](https://www.thelocal.es/20240530/has-spains-housing-law-completely-failed-to-control-rents), "Has Spain's Housing Law completely failed to control rents?" May 30, 2024; [Spanish Property Insight](https://www.spanishpropertyinsight.com/), "Barcelona considers banning foreign investors and non-residents from housing market," January 25, 2025.

[^21]: [La Moncloa](https://www.lamoncloa.gob.es/lang/en/gobierno/news/Paginas/2026/20260203-international-tourists-spending.aspx), "International tourist spending exceeds €134.7 billion by the end of 2025, 6.8% more than in 2024," February 3, 2026. The broader tourism sector figure (~€260 billion, approximately 16% of GDP) comes from [WTTC](https://wttc.org/news/spain-tourism-sector-could-exceed-260-billion-euros-by-2025) and includes domestic tourism, indirect effects, and induced spending.

[^22]: [El Periódico](http://santacoloma.elperiodico.com/es/economia/20240131/inmigrantes-trabajadores-aportan-mas-seguridad-social-gasto-97559351), "Los trabajadores migrantes aportan 10 veces más a la Seguridad Social de lo que gastan," January 31, 2024. Foreign workers contributed about 10% of Social Security revenue while accounting for roughly 1% of its expenditure. See also [La Moncloa](https://www.lamoncloa.gob.es/lang/en/gobierno/news/Paginas/2024/20241115-foreign-contributors.aspx), "Almost 2.9 million foreign contributors in the Social Security system," November 15, 2024.

[^23]: Spain ended its golden visa program on April 3, 2025 after 12 years. See [France 24](https://www.france24.com/en/europe/20250404-spain-bans-golden-investor-visas-for-non-eu-citizens-in-bid-to-curb-housing-crisis), "Spain bans 'golden' investor visas for non-EU citizens in bid to curb housing crisis," April 4, 2025. [Spanish Property Insight](https://www.spanishpropertyinsight.com/2025/04/15/review-of-the-golden-visa/) reviewed the program's results: Catalonia received nearly 40% of all golden visa investments, with Barcelona the top destination at 7,561 investments.

[^24]: [Ajuntament de Barcelona](https://ajuntament.barcelona.cat/pla-allotjaments-turistics/en), "Special Tourist Accommodation Plan (PEUAT)." The PEUAT divides Barcelona into zones (decline, maintenance, controlled growth) and caps HUT (Habitatge d'Ús Turístic) licenses at zero net growth citywide. The original plan was adopted January 2017; the revised version took full effect after final approval December 2021. See also [Ajuntament de Barcelona](https://ajuntament.barcelona.cat/premsa/2017/01/27/el-peuat-la-primera-regulacio-de-ciutat-per-a-tots-els-allotjaments-turistics/), "El PEUAT, la primera regulació de ciutat per a tots els allotjaments turístics," January 27, 2017.

[^25]: I personally lean toward [open borders](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Open_border) and find [Bryan Caplan's case](https://www.amazon.com/Open-Borders-Science-Ethics-Immigration/dp/1250316960) for them persuasive. For Barcelona, that position is beside the point: the people facing new restrictions are legally present. For the US case, the question is harder to separate. This essay cites fiscal contributions of undocumented workers, but a restrictionist could reasonably reply that enforcing entry policy against people who circumvented it isn't scapegoating. If you believe, as I do, that most immigration restrictions are themselves unjust, then "undocumented" is a legal status imposed by bad rules, not a moral failing. If you don't, the undocumented case requires a different argument than the one this essay makes.
summary:
- The left scapegoats upward (wealthy expats, foreign buyers); the right scapegoats downward (immigrants, asylum seekers). Both directions appear in both countries: Spain has Vox alongside anti-guiri activism; the US has Japanese/Chinese property panics alongside anti-immigration politics.
- The evidence does not support the blame. Housing and wage problems are mostly structural. In Spain, foreign workers contribute 10% of total Social Security revenue while drawing about 1% of its expenditure (driven by a younger age profile), and the broader tourism sector generates roughly 16% of GDP.
- "Guiri" lumps together tourists, digital nomads, and long-term residents, but their impacts differ. The populations doing the most measurable harm (speculative short-term rental operators) are not defined by nationality. The populations defined by nationality (foreign workers, long-term residents) are net fiscal contributors.
- Political power runs the opposite way from wealth. Locals have the vote and regulatory voice at the levels where housing policy is set; EU expats can vote in municipal elections but not regional or national ones; non-EU residents cannot vote at all. Exit costs are asymmetric, but that asymmetry doesn't justify origin-based restrictions.
- "Punching up" is not philosophically defensible. The same logic, "their success is our hardship, restrict them," underpinned antisemitism and the Rwandan genocide. Severity escalates once the principle is accepted, as the Trump administration's ratcheting trajectory across two non-consecutive terms illustrates.
- Market-driven cultural change can feel coercive to people with less purchasing power, but the answer is to address underlying market dynamics, not restrict who can show up.
- The test for regulation vs. scapegoating: does the policy target behavior or origin? Entry policy inherently uses origin; the ethical problem is applying origin-based restrictions to people already participating in the local economy.
- The fixes are deregulatory or at least group-neutral: remove zoning caps, permit bottlenecks, and licensing barriers. Even interventionist solutions (public housing, inclusionary zoning) beat scapegoating because they target the problem, not a group defined by origin.
shareTweet:
Anti-guiri populism in Spain and America First nationalism are the same move. Both blame an out-group defined by origin for structural problems. The evidence doesn't support it. Severity differs; the logic doesn't.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/scapegoating-foreigners-bipartisan-international

title: Tweet
body:
## Tweet

The people I know who embrace AI encounter its problems more directly than anyone. But they have the most faith in human ingenuity to work through them. The real skepticism isn't about the machines. It's about us. https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/ai-skepticism-is-really-about-faith-in-humans

## LinkedIn

I keep noticing the same pattern among friends and family who are skeptical about AI (and previously crypto). It's not that they focus on the limitations. It's that they treat those limitations as reason to disengage entirely.

The enthusiasts I know encounter the same frustrations, but more directly. They build tools, consult with companies, and navigate the ups and downs daily. They get dirtier with the problems than the skeptics ever do.

The paradox: the people who embrace the machines have the most faith in human ingenuity. The skeptics, without realizing it, reveal a lack of confidence in our ability to steer the technology toward something that serves us.

There's no determined future where AI plays any given role. The outcome depends on who shows up to shape it.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/ai-skepticism-is-really-about-faith-in-humans

title: Tweet
body:
## Tweet

I built Ateles, an agentic operating system I use daily. 12+ MCP servers (Gmail, Calendar, Bitcoin wallet, WhatsApp, HomeKit, 1Password, and more) wired into @cursor_ai and CLI agents with persistent rules and reusable skills.

Neotoma is the state layer underneath: 1,000+ contacts, 600 tasks, 170 entity types created by agents, not me. No schemas designed upfront. Every session builds on the last.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-my-agentic-stack-actually-does

## LinkedIn

I built Ateles, an agentic operating system I use daily. 12+ MCP servers (Gmail, Calendar, Bitcoin wallet, WhatsApp, HomeKit, 1Password, and more) wired into Cursor and CLI agents with persistent rules and reusable skills. Email triage, Bitcoin payments, blog posts, website deployments, feedback processing.

Neotoma is the state layer underneath: 1,000+ contacts, 600 tasks, 170 entity types created by agents, not me. No schemas designed upfront. Every session builds on the last.

The architecture separates truth (event-sourced state), strategy (what to do next), and execution (side effects). Today I am the strategy layer. I look at the state, decide priorities, and tell agents to execute. The architecture makes that role replaceable by software.

This post walks through the full Ateles stack, real MCP payloads, and what running this daily reveals about what structured agent memory needs to support.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-my-agentic-stack-actually-does

title: I joined Blockstack in 2018 for the developer tools. Gaia, the distributed stora
body:
I joined Blockstack in 2018 for the developer tools. Gaia, the distributed storage system, was the kind of infrastructure that makes you believe a team has found something real. There was already a small developer community building on it. That was the product surface I signed up to work on.

Over seven years I watched token economics reshape the roadmap. Not through bad intentions. Through incentive structure. Token issuance flips the normal startup sequence: the market capitalizes future narratives before the product exists. The team receives validation before delivery. Belief becomes an asset.

Once that happens, real product signals (active users, retention, developer pain) get replaced by activity proxies: GitHub commits, ecosystem grants, conference presence, partnership announcements. These are internally generated. They do not come from outside the belief system.

The "moment of value" was always 12 to 18 months away. Stacks 2.0, then Stacking rewards, then subnets, then the Nakamoto upgrade, then sBTC. Each was presented as the catalytic event. It never arrived. It only moved.

This is not unique to one company. In 2025, 85% of VC-backed tokens traded below their launch price. The pattern is structural to how token-funded ventures work.

I wrote about what I saw, what it taught me about feedback loops, and why I chose a different path for what I'm building now.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/when-the-chain-becomes-the-product

title: A friend gave me blunt feedback on the Neotoma developer release. He came at it
body:
A friend gave me blunt feedback on the Neotoma developer release. He came at it from a consumer-growth perspective: the kind of thinking shaped by Hormozi value equations, viral landing pages, and Twitter-first distribution. The feedback was honest and well-intentioned. Some of it hit real weaknesses. Some of it applied advice meant for a different product, a different audience, and a different stage.

I ran the full feedback through a structured analysis to separate what applies from what doesn't. The core question: what should I actually change for the developer release, given that Neotoma is an infrastructure primitive and not a consumer app?

## The feedback in context

Isaac's feedback carried a few recurring themes. He saw Neotoma as a vitamin, not a painkiller. He thought the landing page needed more urgency for a common person. He flagged visual incoherence between the goldfish brand element and the 8-bit aesthetic. He said the space feels like a red ocean. He noted he would install if trusted builders vouched for it.

These observations are accurate as surface-level reads. The question is which ones point to real product weaknesses versus misapplied frameworks.

A useful framing from the analysis: Isaac is implicitly optimizing for Twitter AI early adopters, broad curiosity installs, and meme-velocity distribution. The developer release targets a different group entirely: tool-builders, infra-curious engineers, and people already feeling memory drift but lacking language for it.

That gap between his assumed audience and my actual audience explains most of the divergence.

A second builder, Kyran, kicked the tires and gave feedback from a different angle. He ran the CLI, hit an init bug (fixed in 0.3.2), and asked for three things that all land in the apply column: a getting started guide with examples, Docker run instructions ("many would rightly refuse to run this on host machine"), and a visual demo of the CLI with a video or asciinema. He also asked how Neotoma differs from Cline's memory bank—differentiation again. No consumer-growth framing; just friction reduction and trust signals. That consistency across two very different reviewers reinforces that the right moves are clarity and setup, not audience or positioning.

## The full assessment

After layering the Zynga PM lens (behavior loops, manufactured pain, retention over virality) on top of the consumer-growth lens Isaac used, I assessed every component of the feedback. The table below shows which pieces apply, which don't, and how to act on the ones that do without distorting what Neotoma actually is.

| Feedback component | Apply to dev release? | Why (infra / Zynga-PM lens) | How to apply without distortion |
|---|---|---|---|
