| Reduce cognitive load in copy | **Yes** | Devs skim; clarity beats philosophy | 1-sentence invariant + 3 bullets on what breaks without it |
| Make setup feel trivial | **Yes** | Perceived effort kills trial | Show reversible "5-minute happy path" install |
| Sharpen ICP explicitly | **Yes (strongly)** | Narrow ICP increases signal | Name builders debugging agent memory drift in production |
| Use visual metaphor / punchy hero | **Yes (tightened)** | Aids fast parsing | Keep concept; remove visual ambiguity |
| Move from solution-first to use-case-first | **Partially** | Devs want context, not persuasion | Lead with one concrete workflow failure |
| Visual coherence (goldfish / 8-bit mismatch) | **Yes (fix)** | Incoherence weakens authority | Unify metaphor or remove conflicting elements |
| Clean layout / centering / polish | **Yes (light polish)** | Sloppiness lowers trust in infra | Improve spacing + alignment; keep personality |
| Clarify symbolic elements immediately | **Yes** | Confusion increases bounce | Make metaphor legible in <2 seconds |
| Sharper segmentation / persona | **Yes (priority)** | Category clarity prevents "memory tool" bucket | Explicitly exclude non-builders |
| Explicit differentiation from RAG / Obsidian | **Yes (strongly)** | Avoid red-ocean surface classification | Contrast on guarantees, not UI |
| Show why it's structurally 10x better | **Yes (translate)** | Builders need contrast | Frame 10x as reliability, determinism, inspectability |
| Add credible social proof (builder endorsements) | **Yes (strategic)** | Dev adoption is reputation-mediated | Highlight respected builders integrating it |
| Getting started + examples, Docker instructions, CLI demo (video/asciinema) | **Yes** | Builder feedback (Kyran): reduces perceived effort, isolation, and "what does it do?" | Add guide + examples; document Docker run; show CLI with asciinema or short video |
| "Acute pain for common person" framing | **No** | Wrong stage, wrong user | Dev infra pain is not consumer urgency |
| Viral landing-page style | **No** | Optimizes installs, not serious adoption | Use sober, inspectable design |
| Hormozi value equation framing | **No** | Offer math does not apply to infra adoption | Optimize long-term trust loops |
| Broad Twitter AI targeting | **No** | High noise, low retention | Focus on builders with systems debt |
| Competing via slogans ("own your memory") | **No** | Empty without falsifiable claims | Replace with invariant + guarantees |
| Overreact to "red ocean" fear | **No** | Infra always looks crowded early | Win via structural differentiation |
| Shift toward meme virality to stand out | **No** | Undermines seriousness | Stand out via conceptual clarity |
| Chase public engagement metrics | **No** | Views are not builder adoption | Measure installs + integration depth |
| Broaden to "common AI user" | **No** | Dilutes signal quality | Keep dev-only framing |

The split is roughly 13 yes, 9 no. That ratio matters. It means the feedback is not wrong overall. It just needs filtering through the lens of what this product actually is and who it's for right now.

## Reduce cognitive load in copy

This is one of the cleanest hits in the feedback. The current materials assume careful reading, shared mental models, and patience. Even developers don't behave that way on first contact.

The hero section needs one crisp promise at the top: what class of failure Neotoma prevents. Then bullets that answer "what breaks without this?" That's not dumbing down. It's reducing parse time.

The target users think in failure modes. They ask: what breaks, under what conditions, how do I debug this, can I reproduce state? Speaking their language in the first five seconds is the difference between someone reading the docs and someone bouncing.

I don't need to simplify the system. I need to compress the cognitive entry point. The depth stays. The first impression gets faster.

## Make setup feel trivial

Isaac is right that the current messaging signals high commitment: architectural buy-in, conceptual overhead, long-term thinking. For a developer release, that destroys impulse adoption.

The actual setup is lighter than it reads. The materials need to reflect that. A "happy path" install that looks reversible. Language like: point X at Y, run Z, stop anytime. Even if the system is deep, the first contact must feel light.

The target ICP will tolerate more friction than a consumer audience. They accept setup steps, configuration, reading docs, and imperfect UX. What they won't tolerate: magical claims, black boxes, and unclear guarantees. So "make it look trivial" must not become "make it sound trivial." The goal is reducing perceived effort without sacrificing credibility.

## Sharpen ICP explicitly

This is the highest-priority item because it governs everything downstream: messaging, content, distribution channels, and what success looks like.

Right now the developer release materials say "developers building agents." That's too broad. It captures people using agents for one-off prompts, people building chatbots, people experimenting casually. None of those are the target.

The real ICP is narrower: builders debugging agent memory drift in production, people maintaining long-running agent workflows, teams watching context degrade across sessions. These people already feel the problem. They build workarounds: ad-hoc files, context stuffing, fragile JSON blobs, manual hallucination correction. They think in failure modes, not features.

I need the materials to name this audience explicitly. If someone reads the first paragraph and doesn't recognize their own pain, the page should let them leave. Attracting the wrong users at this stage creates noise in feedback, inflates support burden, and distorts signal quality.

Isaac's feedback pushes toward broadening the audience. The correct move for a developer release is the opposite: narrow hard, serve the people who already feel this, and let the product harden before expanding.

## Use visual metaphor / punchy hero

This one I'm applying with a modification. Isaac wants a punchy, attention-grabbing hero. The instinct is right: the hero needs to parse fast. But "punchy" in infra doesn't mean the same thing as "punchy" in consumer products.

For the developer release, a visual metaphor that communicates the core idea in under two seconds is valuable. But it needs to aid comprehension, not replace it. The visual should prime the reader for what they're about to learn, not substitute for the content itself.

I'm keeping the concept of a strong visual anchor. I'm removing anything that reads as ambiguous or decorative without purpose. The metaphor must be legible instantly or it adds friction instead of reducing it.

## Move from solution-first to use-case-first

This one I'm applying partially. Isaac is right that leading with the solution ("Neotoma is a structured memory layer") puts the burden on the reader to figure out why they should care. Leading with a concrete workflow failure is stronger for first contact.

But I'm not going all the way to "acute pain for common person." The use case needs to be specific to the ICP: an agent that gradually degrades over sessions, context that silently drifts, a hallucination that trace-back reveals was caused by stale memory. Walk one failure end-to-end. Show where Neotoma intervenes.

The Zynga PM logic here is useful: close one loop before expanding. One narrow, real workflow failure is worth more than five abstract value propositions.

## Visual coherence (goldfish / 8-bit mismatch)

Isaac flagged the goldfish and 8-bit mismatch. This isn't about pixel preferences. It's about narrative coherence, signal precision, and trust calibration.

Infra primitives need an intentional aesthetic with internal logic consistency. Playful is fine. Random is not. If the visual metaphors don't align, if buttons don't map clearly to concepts, if there's symbolic ambiguity, it reads as conceptual looseness.

The audience values inspectability and intentionality. They read schemas, browse state, diff changes, and ask "where did this come from?" A visually incoherent landing page sends the wrong signal to exactly the people I'm trying to reach.

I can keep the 8-bit framing and the "agent dementia" tension. But the visual system needs to cohere. The fix is unifying the metaphor or removing the conflicting elements. Clarity is authority.

## Clean layout / centering / polish

Light polish matters for infra credibility. This doesn't mean a design overhaul. It means spacing, alignment, and intentional layout. Sloppy formatting signals sloppy engineering in the developer's mind.

This is a low-effort, high-return fix. The personality stays. The craftsmanship becomes visible. I'm applying this but not over-investing. The product matters more than the landing page.

## Clarify symbolic elements immediately

If someone lands on the page and can't parse the visual metaphor in under two seconds, it increases bounce. This is related to visual coherence but more specific: every symbolic element (icons, illustrations, layout cues) needs to be self-explanatory on first glance.

For the developer release, this means any visual that requires explanation is a liability. Either it communicates instantly or it goes. The audience is not going to study the hero image. They'll glance at it, make a judgment, and scroll.

## Sharper segmentation / persona

This is closely related to sharpening the ICP but operates at the messaging layer. It's not enough to know the ICP internally. The materials need to say who this is for and who it is not for.

Explicitly excluding non-builders is important. If someone recognizes themselves as excluded, they leave. That's good. The alternative is ambiguous targeting where everyone tries it and nobody integrates it.

Category clarity prevents the "memory tool" bucket. If the materials don't draw the line, readers will draw their own, and they'll place Neotoma next to Obsidian and note-taking apps. That's a fatal misread for an infra primitive.

## Explicit differentiation from RAG / Obsidian

Isaac said the space feels like a red ocean. He's reacting to repeated language (memory, RAG, context, agents), similar surface UX, and high social media density. That reaction is valid but misdiagnosed.

The ocean looks red because surface affordances are similar and the underlying abstraction layer isn't settled yet. Historically, infra categories always look overcrowded before they consolidate. Databases in 2008, cloud providers in 2011, CI/CD tools in 2014. All looked saturated. One or two players defined the architecture. The rest faded.

The real risk isn't that there are many memory tools. The risk is being categorized as one of them. The materials need to make the category break visible. Not "own your memory" or "infinite memory." Instead: deterministic ingestion, schema-first state, auditable memory evolution. Contrast explicitly with prompt stuffing, vector DB hacks, and file-based RAG memory. Contrast on guarantees, not UI.

## Show why it's structurally 10x better

Isaac noted he'd install if trusted people said it was 10x better. The 10x claim matters but needs translation for infra. In consumer products, 10x means faster, prettier, more delightful. In infrastructure, 10x means: it doesn't silently break, I can trace what happened, my system is reliable after 30 days not just day one.

The "10x better" framing I should use: reliability instead of performance, determinism instead of speed, inspectability instead of polish. These are the dimensions where Neotoma actually is structurally different. The materials need to make that contrast concrete and falsifiable.

## Add credible social proof (builder endorsements)

Developer adoption is reputation-mediated. Isaac explicitly said he'd install if respected people vouched for it. That's standard for infra tools. Nobody adopts a new database because of a clever landing page. They adopt it because someone they trust integrated it and it held up.

For the developer release, this means prioritizing 5 strong builder endorsements over 500 likes. Case study threads over launch hype. Real integration stories over feature lists. The propagation channel for infra is DMs and "you should look at this" messages, not public retweets.

I'm applying this strategically because the social proof needs to be credible. A few respected builders integrating Neotoma and sharing their experience is worth more than manufactured testimonials. This takes time but compounds.

## "Acute pain for common person" framing

Not applying. "Why does my life get better tomorrow?" is not the right question for infra. Infra adoption runs on failure avoidance, regression prevention, and system reliability. Applying consumer pain language would attract the wrong users and force dishonest simplification.

The developer release audience doesn't need to be sold on why memory matters. They already know. They need to be shown why this approach to memory is structurally different from what they've tried. Consumer urgency framing undermines that conversation.

## Viral landing-page style

Not applying. 90s video game graphics, slogans, and meme-y punchiness optimize for clicks and social sharing. They harm trust, inspectability, and perceived seriousness.

A Zynga PM used virality inside the product, not in infra marketing. The landing page should be sober, inspectable, and precise. The product is where the habit loop forms. The landing page just needs to earn a trial.

## Hormozi value equation framing

Not applying. The value equation works for services, offers, and direct response funnels. It breaks for infrastructure, protocols, and long-horizon tools. Neotoma is a system primitive that compounds over time. Applying transactional offer math would push toward overpromising and shallow differentiation.

The Zynga PM reframe is useful here: Zynga didn't reduce time to dream outcome. They reduced time to habit formation. Different math. The relevant optimization for Neotoma is how fast a builder integrates and starts trusting the system, not how fast they see a discrete result.

## Broad Twitter AI targeting

Not applying. Twitter's AI audience is curious, overexposed, and low-retention. For a developer release, that's poison. The better audience is fewer users with higher systems maturity and willingness to integrate and reflect.

The engagement pattern I'm seeing (low public reaction, high private discussion) is actually the correct signal for an infra primitive. Infra tools spread via personal recommendations, repo links, and "you should look at this" messages. Not via public hype cycles.

## Competing via slogans ("own your memory")

Not applying. Slogans without falsifiable claims are empty. "Own your memory" doesn't differentiate from ten other products making the same claim. Replacing slogans with invariants and guarantees (deterministic ingestion, schema-first state, auditable evolution) gives builders something they can verify.

The developer release should make claims that can be tested and disproven. That's what builds trust with the ICP. Slogans do the opposite: they sound good and mean nothing.

## Overreact to "red ocean" fear

Not applying. Infra always looks crowded early because the problem is real and the abstraction layer isn't settled. The ocean is surface-red, not structurally red. Noise is evidence of instability, not saturation.

The question is not "how crowded is memory?" The question is "what invariant do you define that others don't?" If that invariant is strong, the ocean color is irrelevant. Red ocean anxiety should not drive repositioning.

## Shift toward meme virality to stand out

Not applying. The "stand out" instinct can mutate into over-marketing, over-promising, and trend-chasing. Infra tools lose trust quickly if they smell like that.

Standing out in infra means conceptual clarity, not louder messaging. The goal is to be the tool that builders discover, evaluate seriously, and integrate quietly. Not the tool that trends for a day and gets forgotten.

## Chase public engagement metrics

Not applying. Views, likes, and impressions are vanity metrics for a developer release. The metrics that matter: who reads the docs fully, who installs and comes back, who sends questions after days.

The success behavior for the developer release is not installs or signups. It's this: after two weeks, a builder trusts their system more and feels uneasy turning Neotoma off. That's when the vitamin begins converting to a painkiller. No landing page metric captures that.

## Broaden to "common AI user"

Not applying. Neotoma is not for a "common person" yet. Forcing that framing now would dilute conceptual sharpness, attract low-signal users, and increase support and confusion.

The Zynga PM logic: start with whales, not civilians. Let the system harden before mass appeal. The developer release should serve the narrowest audience that generates the highest-quality signal. Everyone else can come later.

## The synthesis

Isaac's feedback is not wrong. It's mis-scoped. The thirteen items I'm applying all reduce friction, increase legibility, strengthen differentiation, or build trust with serious builders. The nine I'm rejecting all expand audience prematurely, optimize for virality, dilute conceptual sharpness, or force consumer urgency framing.

The developer release should feel narrow, serious, precise, and slightly demanding. If it feels mass-appeal ready, it's misaligned.

The actionable kernel from the Zynga PM analysis is the most useful reframe: I haven't yet designed the moment where users feel worse without Neotoma. That's a product problem, not a landing page problem. And it's the right problem to solve next.
summary:
- Consumer-growth feedback on an infra-first developer release is useful for friction reduction but misleading for audience and positioning decisions.
- Of 22 feedback components assessed (Isaac + Kyran), 13 apply to the developer release and 9 do not. The split tracks whether feedback reduces friction or changes the nature of the user or the promise.
- The real risk for Neotoma is not red ocean competition but category misplacement as "another memory tool" instead of a state correctness layer.
- Feedback that expands audience prematurely, optimizes for virality, or forces consumer urgency framing is incompatible with a developer release for infrastructure.
- The most useful reframe: the product problem is designing the moment users feel worse without it, not improving landing page copy.
shareTweet:
Got early feedback on the Neotoma developer release from a consumer-growth lens. Some of it hit real weaknesses. Some was for a different product entirely.

Wrote up what I'm keeping, what I'm discarding, and why the distinction matters for infra.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/assessing-isaac-feedback-developer-release

title: # Share copy: When the chain becomes the product
body:
# Share copy: When the chain becomes the product

Copy the block for the platform you're posting to.

---

## Tweet

I joined Blockstack in 2018 for the developer tools. Seven years later I left with a clear picture of what happens when token dynamics replace product feedback loops. Not a failure of people. A failure of incentive structure.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/when-the-chain-becomes-the-product

---

## LinkedIn

I joined Blockstack in 2018 for the developer tools. Gaia, the distributed storage system, was the kind of infrastructure that makes you believe a team has found something real. There was already a small developer community building on it. That was the product surface I signed up to work on.

Over seven years I watched token economics reshape the roadmap. Not through bad intentions. Through incentive structure. Token issuance flips the normal startup sequence: the market capitalizes future narratives before the product exists. The team receives validation before delivery. Belief becomes an asset.

Once that happens, real product signals (active users, retention, developer pain) get replaced by activity proxies: GitHub commits, ecosystem grants, conference presence, partnership announcements. These are internally generated. They do not come from outside the belief system.

The "moment of value" was always 12 to 18 months away. Stacks 2.0, then Stacking rewards, then subnets, then the Nakamoto upgrade, then sBTC. Each was presented as the catalytic event. It never arrived. It only moved.

This is not unique to one company. In 2025, 85% of VC-backed tokens traded below their launch price. The pattern is structural to how token-funded ventures work.

I wrote about what I saw, what it taught me about feedback loops, and why I chose a different path for what I'm building now.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/when-the-chain-becomes-the-product

---

## Hacker News

**Title (sentence case):** When the chain becomes the product: Seven years inside a token-funded venture

**URL:** https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/when-the-chain-becomes-the-product

title: # LinkedIn share (draft)
body:
# LinkedIn share (draft)

I built Ateles, an agentic operating system I use daily. 12+ MCP servers (Gmail, Calendar, Bitcoin wallet, WhatsApp, HomeKit, 1Password, and more) wired into Cursor and CLI agents with persistent rules and reusable skills. Email triage, Bitcoin payments, blog posts, website deployments, feedback processing.

Neotoma is the state layer underneath: 1,000+ contacts, 600 tasks, 170 entity types created by agents, not me. No schemas designed upfront. Every session builds on the last.

The architecture separates truth (event-sourced state), strategy (what to do next), and execution (side effects). Today I am the strategy layer. I look at the state, decide priorities, and tell agents to execute. The architecture makes that role replaceable by software.

This post walks through the full Ateles stack, real MCP payloads, and what running this daily reveals about what structured agent memory needs to support.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-my-agentic-stack-actually-does

title: What my agentic stack actually does
excerpt: I built an agentic stack to dogfood Neotoma and accelerate my work. A private monorepo with 12+ MCP servers where I cowork with AI agents daily. Neotoma provides the structured memory underneath, letting agents build on prior work across every session.
body:
My agentic stack is how I dogfood [Neotoma](https://neotoma.io). It is also my personal operating system. A private monorepo where AI agents handle everything from email triage to Bitcoin payments to website deployments, with Neotoma as the structured memory underneath.

Every feature I ship in Neotoma gets validated here first, primarily in [Cursor](https://cursor.com) and secondarily via terminal agents like [Claude Code](https://www.anthropic.com/claude-code), [Codex](https://openai.com/codex), and [Cursor CLI](https://cursor.com/cli). Every gap I find in agent memory surfaces here first. The stack is how I run my daily life and work. The friction I encounter, in addition to user feedback, is what drives the Neotoma roadmap.

I intend to open-source the stack. But the repo has accumulated months of personal data, scripts hardcoded to my accounts, and configuration tied to my setup. Before it can go public I need to push that data fully into Neotoma and refactor the tooling to be generic. That work is ongoing.

This post explains what the stack is, how I use it, and what it reveals about what structured agent memory needs to do.

## What the stack is

The stack is a monorepo with over a dozen [MCP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io) servers and CLIs, each connecting AI agents to a different service: [Gmail](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-gmail), [Google Calendar](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-google-calendar), [WhatsApp](https://www.whatsapp.com), a [Bitcoin wallet](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-bitcoin), [Instagram](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-instagram), [Asana](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-asana), [HomeKit](https://www.apple.com/home-app-accessories/), [DNSimple](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-dnsimple), [Google Search Console](https://search.google.com/search-console), [1Password](https://1password.com), a [web scraper](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/mcp-server-web-scraper), and more. Some are MCP servers that agents call as tools. Others are CLIs that agents invoke from the terminal. Both give agents the same thing: reach into external services.

On top of the MCP servers sit rules and skills. Rules are persistent behavioral instructions that live in the repo, not in Neotoma: always store contacts in Neotoma before responding, never commit secrets, use sentence case in titles, run tests after code changes, prefer CLI over dashboard for logs and config, link the first mention of product names. Skills are multi-step workflows: triage my inbox, draft a blog post, deploy a website, process product feedback, extract an Amazon order from email, pay a contractor in Bitcoin.

And underneath everything sits [Neotoma](https://neotoma.io) as the structured memory layer. Every agent reads from it and writes to it. That is what makes the stack compound over time instead of resetting on every session.

## How I work with it

I live in Cursor. My day is a sequence of agent sessions. I open a new agent, describe what I want done, and the agent executes using the MCP servers, rules, and skills in the workspace. Some sessions are quick: "reply to this email." Some are long: "triage my inbox, process the latest tester feedback, then draft a comparative post about [Google's memory agent](/posts/always-on-memory-agents-and-the-truth-layer)."

It is coworking with agents. I sit at my desk and work alongside them all day. Personal tasks: scheduling a repair, paying a contractor, managing calendar events. Professional ones: writing posts, processing feedback, deploying websites, managing domains. The agents handle the execution. I provide direction, review output, and approve actions that need a human in the loop.

Each agent session has the full workspace context: every MCP server, every rule, every skill. The agent can read my Gmail, check my calendar, query Neotoma for prior context, store new data, generate images, push code, and verify deployments. My role is increasingly to describe intent and review results.

## How Neotoma fits

Without structured memory, every agent session starts from zero. The agent does not know who your contacts are, what tasks you have open, what you discussed yesterday, or what you already paid someone. You can paste context into every prompt, but that does not scale past a few sessions. [Platform memory](/posts/your-ai-remembers-your-vibe-but-not-your-work) stores your vibe, not your work. [RAG](/posts/why-agent-memory-needs-more-than-rag) helps with code, but not with the structured facts that drive workflows: who you owe money to, what feedback you received last week, which tasks are still open.

My Neotoma instance stores over 1,000 contacts, 600 tasks, 140 conversations, 120 blog posts, and 170 entity types that agents created as they encountered new kinds of information: transactions, standing rules, feedback notes, calendar events, disputes, invoices, skills, deployment results. When an agent starts a new session, it retrieves what it needs. When it finishes, it stores what it learned.

Here are the top 20 entity types in my Neotoma instance today, with an example of each:

| Entity type | Example |
|---|---|
| Agent messages | "triage my inbox and process tester feedback" |
| Commitments | Inform investor if funding strategy changes |
| Companies | Acme Design Studio, acme-studio.com |
| Contacts | Sarah Kim, sarah@example.com, imported from Gmail then extended every time I interact with her |
| Conversations | "Email triage March 9" |
| Disputes | Refund dispute, 99 EUR, in review |
| Email messages | "Re: partnership proposal," March 8 |
| Events | School play rehearsal, Saturday 10am, bring costume |
| Feedback notes | "Schema discovery needs better docs," developer release |
| Invoices | Invoice #2038, 1,450 EUR, home repair |
| Links | LinkedIn, linkedin.com/in/username, social |
| Locations | Barcelona, Spain (home) |
| Posts | Why agent memory needs more than RAG, essay, published |
| Preferences | Never include memo field for pilates class payments |
| Standing rules | Always include transaction link in payment confirmations |
| Skills | fix-feature-bug: classify error, add regression test, run suite; email-triage: process inbox, draft replies, archive |
| Tasks | "Buy resistance bands," pending with urgency level, health domain |
| Timeline entries | Project Manager at TechCrunch, 2007-2009 |
| Transactions | 0.0021 BTC ($178) paid to Carlos for March services |

None of these schemas were designed upfront. Agents [create and extend schemas as needed](https://neotoma.io) as they encounter new kinds of information. The system now has 170 entity types total, most with only a handful of records. The long tail is where agent memory gets interesting: a single dispute entity with its full negotiation history, a single commitment to follow up with someone if strategy changes, a single preference about how to handle a specific payment.

The practical difference is that agents build on prior work. When I ask an agent to email someone, it searches Neotoma for the contact first. When I ask it to process feedback, it retrieves existing feedback entities and links new ones. When I ask it to pay a contractor in Bitcoin, it knows the standing rule (always pay this person in BTC) and includes the transaction link in the confirmation because another standing rule says to.

## Storage and retrieval up close

The Neotoma MCP exposes tools that agents call directly. Here is what real storage and retrieval looks like in practice.

When an agent needs to persist a conversation turn with extracted entities, it calls `store` with a single payload:

```json
store({
  "entities": [
    {
      "entity_type": "conversation",
      "title": "Email triage March 9"
    },
    {
      "entity_type": "agent_message",
      "role": "user",
      "content": "triage my inbox",
      "turn_key": "conv-42:1"
    },
    {
      "entity_type": "contact",
      "full_name": "Alex Chen",
      "email": "alex@example.com",
      "source": "tester feedback call"
    },
    {
      "entity_type": "task",
      "title": "Follow up on Alex's feedback",
      "status": "pending",
      "priority": "medium"
    }
  ],
  "relationships": [
    {
      "relationship_type": "PART_OF",
      "source_index": 1,
      "target_index": 0
    },
    {
      "relationship_type": "REFERS_TO",
      "source_index": 1,
      "target_index": 2
    },
    {
      "relationship_type": "REFERS_TO",
      "source_index": 1,
      "target_index": 3
    }
  ],
  "idempotency_key": "conv-42-turn-1-triage"
})
```

One call stores the conversation, the message, a new contact, and a task, all linked by typed relationships. The agent did not need a schema definition for `contact` or `task` beforehand. Neotoma accepts arbitrary fields and infers structure.

When an agent needs context before responding, it queries by identifier:

```json
retrieve_entity_by_identifier({
  "identifier": "Alex Chen"
})
```

This returns the contact record with email, prior conversations, and the provenance of each field. If the agent needs broader context, it queries by type:

```json
retrieve_entities({
  "entity_type": "feedback_note",
  "search": "developer release",
  "limit": 10
})
```

This returns the ten most relevant feedback notes about the developer release, each with its full snapshot and observation history.

For standing rules, the agent retrieves them once at the start of a workflow:

```json
retrieve_entities({
  "entity_type": "standing_rule"
})
```

My instance returns rules like "always pay Carlos in Bitcoin" and "provide drafted messages in markdown blocks." The agent applies these automatically for the rest of the session.

When workflows touch files (receipts, screenshots, documents), the agent stores them alongside entities in the same call using `file_path`:

```json
store({
  "entities": [
    {
      "entity_type": "transaction",
      "vendor": "Amazon",
      "amount": 47.99,
      "currency": "EUR"
    }
  ],
  "file_path": "/path/to/receipt.pdf",
  "idempotency_key": "amazon-order-march-9"
})
```

Neotoma's [unstructured storage path](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) handles the file from there. The raw bytes are content-addressed (SHA-256) so the same file is never stored twice. The agent passes the file as-is via `file_path` (local environments like Cursor) or `file_content` (base64, for web-based environments); it does not interpret or extract data before storing. By default, Neotoma runs AI interpretation on the stored file automatically, extracting structured entities and linking them back to the source with an EMBEDS relationship. Re-storing the same file with `interpret: true` triggers reinterpretation without creating a duplicate. The receipt becomes queryable structured data with the original PDF preserved for provenance. Interpretation can also be deferred (`interpret: false`) for batch processing or quota management, then run later with different configuration.

## Workflows in practice

A few workflows show the pattern.

**Email triage.** The agent reads unread emails via Gmail MCP, checks Neotoma for existing contact records and prior context with each sender, drafts replies using my communication style rules, stores new contacts and tasks, and archives processed messages. A single triage run might store five new contacts, three tasks, and a dozen conversation turns.

**Blog post writing.** The skill itself lives in Neotoma as a structured entity. The agent retrieves it with `retrieve_entity_snapshot`. It then queries existing posts for style calibration, writes the draft, stores the post entity with all metadata, generates hero images, creates share copy for Twitter and LinkedIn, regenerates the website cache from the Neotoma export, and deploys. This post was written that way.

**Bitcoin payments.** I pay a contractor in Bitcoin using [a BTC wallet MCP server](/posts/agentic-wallets-mcp-bitcoin). Neotoma stores the standing rule, the contact record, and the transaction history. The agent retrieves all three, executes the payment, stores the new transaction with the on-chain link, and confirms.

**Feedback processing.** When testers give feedback on the Neotoma developer release, agents extract structured feedback entities, link them to the tester's contact record, classify the feedback by bucket, and assess it against release stage constraints. Prior feedback is retrievable by tester, by bucket, or by date.

**Website deployment.** The deploy skill syncs local markdown edits to Neotoma, exports the full website dataset, regenerates the cache, pushes the [website repo](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/markmhendrickson), and monitors [GitHub Actions](https://github.com/features/actions) until the build succeeds. If the build fails, the agent reads the logs, fixes the issue, and re-runs.

## Toward agents that run without me

Every workflow starts with me opening a Cursor agent and typing an instruction. I am in the loop for every task. That is fine for the current stage, but it is not the end state.

I am setting up automated processes so agents can handle workflows without my direct involvement. The pieces are already there: skills define the full workflow steps, Neotoma stores the context and rules, MCP servers provide the reach. What is missing is the orchestration that triggers workflows on schedule or in response to events, and a lightweight approval interface so I can review and authorize actions without sitting at my laptop.

Neotoma's [layered architecture](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma/blob/dev/docs/foundation/layered_architecture.md) is designed for exactly this. It separates three concerns:

1. **Truth layer (Neotoma).** Event-sourced, reducer-driven, deterministic. All agents read from it. State updates flow only through domain events processed by reducers. No agent mutates truth directly.
2. **Strategy layer.** Reads the current world state from Neotoma. Evaluates priorities, constraints, risk, commitments, and time. Outputs decisions and commands. Pure cognition: state in, decisions out. No side effects.
3. **Execution layer.** Takes commands from the strategy layer. Performs side effects through external adapters (email APIs, payment services, calendar, messaging). Emits domain events describing what happened. Those events flow back through reducers to update state. Pure effect: commands in, events out.

The loop is closed:

```
Inbound signals (email, WhatsApp, calendar, financial data)
  -> Normalization -> Neotoma state (event log + reducers)
  -> Strategy tick (evaluate priorities, output decisions)
  -> Execution agents (perform side effects, emit events)
  -> Reducers -> Updated state
  -> Next tick
```

Today, I am the strategy layer. I look at the state, decide what to do, and tell an agent to execute. The architecture makes that role replaceable by software. A strategy engine reads Neotoma, evaluates what needs attention based on standing rules and priorities, and issues commands to execution agents. Those agents call MCP servers, store the results, and the cycle repeats.

The critical invariant is that no layer writes to Neotoma's underlying data store directly. Updates only flow through domain events and reducers. That makes the system auditable and reversible. If an autonomous agent makes a bad decision, I can trace the event that caused it, revert the state update, and correct the rule that led to it.

The goal is to reduce my daily time at the computer. Not eliminate it. Shift from hands-on execution to review and approval. I want to wake up to a summary of what my agents handled overnight: emails triaged, posts drafted, deployments verified, payments queued. I want to approve a Bitcoin payment from my [Apple Watch](https://www.apple.com/watch). I want to review a drafted email on my phone while walking and tap to send. The agents handle the 80% that is repeatable. I handle the 20% that needs judgment.

This is where the hardware question gets interesting. Today's phones and watches are not designed for this interaction pattern. You need a device optimized for brief review and approval gestures, not for typing or browsing.

Of the devices that exist today, the [Apple Watch](https://www.apple.com/watch) feels closest to the right form factor: always on your wrist, glanceable, capable of simple tap-to-approve interactions. But the software layer is not there yet. There is no way to pipe agent summaries and approval requests to the watch in a way that feels native.

That might be an area for me to experiment with at some point, building a lightweight companion app that bridges Neotoma's state to a wrist-level interface. Whether the right surface ends up being a watch app, a dedicated AI device, or something that does not exist yet, the interaction model is clear: agents do the work, structured memory holds the state, and the human provides direction at the pace of intent rather than the pace of execution.

## Open-sourcing the stack

The stack is private today because it contains my life: contacts, finances, health data, personal communications, standing rules about how I run my household. Before I can open-source it, I need to untangle all of that.

The path is straightforward. Personal data moves fully into Neotoma, which is already the source of truth for most of it. Scripts that reference my specific accounts and paths get refactored to read from configuration. MCP server wrappers become generic. Skills lose their hardcoded assumptions.

What remains is a reusable agentic stack: a monorepo template with MCP server scaffolding, a rules and skills framework, Neotoma integration for structured memory, and example workflows that anyone can adapt. The architecture is the interesting part. My personal data is not.

I do not have a timeline for this. The refactoring happens alongside the daily use. Every time I touch a script, I make it more generic. Every time I move data into Neotoma, I remove it from the repo. The stack gets more portable with each session.

## What this proves about Neotoma

I built this stack before Neotoma existed. The early version used flat files and [Parquet](https://parquet.apache.org) tables. It worked until it did not.

The [failure modes](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) were specific: an agent would store a contact as "Sarah Kim" in one session and "S. Kim" in another, creating duplicates with no way to merge them. There was no provenance, so I couldn't tell which agent wrote a field or when.

Queries were limited to exact matches on single columns, so asking "what feedback did I get last week?" meant scanning every file manually. Occasionally records were overwritten badly or deleted entirely, with no event log to recover from. And nothing linked across types, so knowing that a task related to a contact related to a transaction required me to hold that graph in my head.

Neotoma replaced that layer. It gave agents a [structured, queryable, relationship-aware memory](/posts/agent-memory-truth-problem) that works across every workflow. The stack now has 170 entity types in Neotoma, not because I designed 170 schemas upfront, but because agents create entity types as they encounter new kinds of information. A feedback note is different from a transaction is different from a standing rule, and the system handles all of them.

This is the dogfooding that keeps Neotoma honest. When retrieval is slow, I feel it in every agent session. When entity resolution fails, I get duplicate contacts. When storage is unreliable, workflows break. Every bug and every gap shows up in my daily work before it shows up in anyone else's.

The memory problem is universal. Every developer building agentic workflows will hit the same wall: agents that cannot remember, cannot query, and cannot build on prior work. [Retrieval alone is not enough](/posts/why-agent-memory-needs-more-than-rag); structure and provenance are what make memory trustworthy. This stack is proof that structured memory changes what agents can do. Neotoma is how I am making that available to everyone.

The [developer release](/posts/neotoma-developer-release) is open for testing. If you are building agentic workflows and want structured memory underneath, that is where to start.
summary:
- My agentic stack is a private monorepo with 12+ MCP servers, persistent rules, and reusable skills. I cowork with AI agents in Cursor all day, opening sessions for tasks from email triage to Bitcoin payments to website deployments.
- Neotoma provides the structured memory layer underneath: 1,000+ contacts, 600 tasks, 170 entity types. Agents store entities and relationships on every turn and retrieve prior context before responding.
- Real MCP calls show the pattern: a single `store` call persists a conversation, extracted contacts, tasks, and typed relationships in one payload. Retrieval calls give agents context before they act.
- Neotoma's layered architecture separates truth (event-sourced state), strategy (pure cognition, decisions out), and execution (side effects, events back). Today I am the strategy layer. The architecture makes that role replaceable by software.
- The goal is to shift from hands-on execution to review and approval, with agents handling repeatable workflows autonomously. The stack will be open-sourced once personal data is migrated into Neotoma and scripts are refactored to be generic.
shareTweet:
I built Ateles, an agentic operating system I use daily. 12+ MCP servers (Gmail, Calendar, Bitcoin wallet, WhatsApp, HomeKit, 1Password, and more) wired into @cursor_ai and CLI agents with persistent rules and reusable skills.

Neotoma is the state layer underneath: 1,000+ contacts, 600 tasks, 170 entity types created by agents, not me. No schemas designed upfront. Every session builds on the last.

https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/what-my-agentic-stack-actually-does

title: Spain is at a crossroads on housing and migration
excerpt: Spain can remain open and still protect affordability, but only if housing policy and migration policy are designed as one system.
body:
## Spain is at a crossroads

Spain has become a magnet for mobile workers, retirees, digital professionals, and investors looking for quality of life. That inflow brings spending, talent, and business activity. It also raises pressure in neighborhoods where housing supply has not kept pace.

The result is visible in major cities and coastal areas: rent inflation, conversion of long term housing into short term use, and rising frustration among residents who feel pushed out of their own communities. The public debate is no longer abstract. It is about whether Spain can stay open while protecting social stability.

## What the current moment looks like

The broad pattern is clear:

- Demand for housing in high demand areas is rising faster than practical supply.
- Productive foreign demand is mixing with speculative demand.
- Local wages are not keeping pace with housing costs in many markets.
- Enforcement capacity remains uneven across municipalities.
- Policy signals are often fragmented, which creates uncertainty for residents and investors.

This is the core contradiction. Spain wants growth, tax base expansion, and international talent. Spain also needs affordability, local trust, and political legitimacy. Both goals are valid. The current policy mix is not strong enough to deliver both at once.

## Why the United States migration story matters

Recent reporting on Americans leaving the United States highlights an important point: migration pressure into countries like Spain is structural, not temporary ([WSJ](https://www.wsj.com/us-news/americans-leaving-the-us-migration-a5795bfa?mod=mhp)). People are making long term life moves based on cost, safety, healthcare, and lifestyle.

That means Spain should plan for a durable inflow, not a short cycle. A temporary response to a structural trend will keep producing the same conflict.

## A practical policy package for Spain

Spain does not need a single silver bullet. It needs a coordinated package with clear metrics and visible enforcement.

### 1) Rebalance housing supply toward long term residents

Create stronger incentives for long term rental contracts and owner occupancy. Pair these incentives with faster permitting for new residential units in places where public services can support growth.

### 2) Tighten rules for speculative and extractive demand

Use differentiated taxation and compliance checks to discourage purely speculative purchases that leave units underused. Keep the door open for productive investment that adds real housing stock or local jobs.

### 3) Set enforceable standards for short term rentals

Require licensing, enforce caps by zone where saturation is high, and fund audits with real penalties for noncompliance. Rules without enforcement will not change outcomes.

### 4) Link migration pathways to local integration outcomes

For incoming residents on high earning or investment pathways, align policy with local contribution goals: tax compliance, long term residence intent, and participation in the formal economy.

### 5) Build a local affordability compact

Channel part of migration linked tax revenue into municipal housing funds, transit, and neighborhood services in the same areas experiencing pressure. People need to see direct reinvestment where costs are rising.

### 6) Publish a national dashboard

Track rent burden, vacancy by use type, licensing compliance, and displacement risk by district. Public visibility helps depoliticize claims and allows faster policy correction.

## The political principle

Spain does not need to choose between openness and social cohesion. It needs to govern openness with better market design and more credible institutions.

An open country without affordability loses legitimacy. A protected housing market without growth loses dynamism. The viable path is disciplined openness: welcome people, reward productive contribution, and protect the conditions for residents to remain rooted.

## Why this framing now

This post builds on the argument in this shared discussion on socialist countries and gentrification ([ChatGPT share](https://chatgpt.com/share/69a1a056-ec0c-8012-9589-08b58215ec6c)) and extends it with a Spain specific action plan.

The key takeaway is simple. The question is no longer whether these pressures are real. The question is whether policy can move faster than displacement.
summary:
- Spain is facing a structural housing pressure problem, not a temporary cycle.
- Migration demand can support growth, but unmanaged demand can accelerate displacement.
- Spain needs a coordinated policy package that links housing supply, rental enforcement, and migration design.
- Local legitimacy depends on visible reinvestment in neighborhoods under the most pressure.
- The goal is disciplined openness that protects social cohesion while preserving economic dynamism.

title: Where agents and humans meet: coordination and the truth layer
excerpt: The bottleneck for agentic adoption is not raw capability but shared state and legibility. A truth layer is the coordination substrate both sides can rely on.
body:
Two recent threads sharpened how I think about this: one on [AI company funding](https://chatgpt.com/share/69931e04-92c4-8012-9389-83ab7aa1106b) (who has raised what, and why Neotoma does not appear on those lists) and one on [AI in enterprise IT](https://chatgpt.com/share/699bfb11-6b58-8012-a743-66ea0e8ba8d4) (Ed Sim's LP notes on power-law concentration, the software reset, and where durable value accrues). In both, the same pattern showed up. Funding tables measure capital consumption: model labs, infrastructure, AI-native products. Enterprise discourse is about agent orchestration, bottom-up adoption, and what survives when "skills" become atomic. The quiet subtext in both is that **memory, coordination, and trust layers** are first-class infrastructure, not features. Control planes (memory, identity, permissions, provenance) may outlast the agent frameworks built on top of them.

This post is about how I think about that coordination in the context of [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma): the [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) I'm building. The goal is not to replace agents or humans. It is to give both a shared place where state lives, where it is legible, and where handoffs between sessions and tools do not drop the ball. The infrastructure that wins is the one that makes coordination explicit: one store, one set of entities, one history. And for a growing segment, that store has to be a substrate you own, not context inside someone else's product.

## Why coordination shows up in funding and enterprise

When people talk about AI companies that are raising money or landing in enterprises, the themes repeat. Trust. Audit. Who changed what, and when. Can we roll back. Can we prove it. Those are not model questions. They are state and coordination questions.

In the funding chat I asked where Neotoma fits. The answer was that it does not fit on those lists at all. The lists measure who is burning capital to chase intelligence. Neotoma is about making intelligence accountable. Different game. The right comparison set is not AI companies but foundational coordination technologies: Git, SQLite, Bitcoin Core. They raised little or no capital, defined the constraints within which much larger systems later operated, and won by being boring, correct, and inevitable. Neotoma sits below model labs and applications. It is the substrate; agents and workspaces sit on top.

In the enterprise chat the same split appeared. One take was that "your memory project is a future feature that will be subsumed by the platforms." The counter is structural: once memory is durable, inspectable, user-owned, and cross-agent, it stops being a feature and becomes infrastructure. Platform-owned convenience memory is a feature. User-owned, inspectable, interoperable memory resists enclosure. Platforms are good at the first and historically weak at the second when it threatens lock-in. So the coordination layer is not something the big players will "ship" and absorb. It is the thing that has to exist for agents and humans to collaborate without depending on a single vendor's context.

Agents that act in the world need to read and write state. Humans need to inspect that state, correct it, and approve what matters. If the agent's view of reality and the human's view of reality drift apart, collaboration breaks. If there is no single place that both sides treat as the source of truth, you get duplicate records, conflicting facts, and handoffs that lose context when you switch from one tool to another. The infrastructure that matters is the one that makes coordination explicit: one store, one set of entities, one history. That is what a truth layer is for.

## What Neotoma adds to the coordination story

Neotoma is the context substrate you own. Typed entities, canonical IDs, full provenance, and access via MCP so that the same store is available to every agent and every session. It is not a workspace or a hub; it is the data layer. In the funding chat the analogy that fit was Git: Git answers "what actually happened to this code, in what order, by whom?" Neotoma answers "what actually happened in this system or life, in what order, from which sources?" Everything else (agents, IDEs, copilots) sits on top. That is the post-agent coordination problem: multiple agents, multiple models, multiple timelines, no shared ground truth. Coordination is baked in because both sides read and write the same graph. Comment threads on agent infrastructure (e.g. the recent Notion COO post on context and legibility) keep landing on the same split: workspace-hosted context versus "I want to own that." Neotoma is for the second. You own the layer; workspaces can consume or display it, but the canonical store is not inside someone else's infrastructure.

**One state, many actors.** Agents do not each keep their own copy of "what we know." They read from the same store. When one agent (or one session) creates a task, updates a contact, or records a decision, the next agent or the next day sees that update. No "remember last time we talked about X." The record is just there. Humans see the same record. So agents and humans are coordinated by construction, as long as they both use the same layer. The memory graph (entities, relationships, events) is what makes context richness possible: structured, entity-resolved, cross-document context without dumping token noise into every request. That is the context graph that commenters keep pointing to as the real bottleneck for agents.

**Legibility.** The store is not opaque embeddings or session-only context. It is structured data: entities, relationships, events. You can query it, inspect it, and see exactly what was stored and when. That means a human can look at what an agent did and correct it. Legibility is what makes collaboration possible. Without it, the human is blind and the agent might as well be writing to a black box. One commenter in that thread put it well: capability only matters if humans can verify what the agent did and why. A shared store with full provenance gives you that. The other half is authority (what the agent is allowed to do); a truth layer does not build the full permissions product, but row-level security and a complete audit trail of who wrote what lay the groundwork so governance can sit on top.

**Provenance and rollback.** Every change is an explicit operation with a source and a timestamp. You can trace where a fact came from and, when something goes wrong, you can reason about what to undo. That matters for enterprise: compliance and audit require "how did we get here." It also matters for trust. If an agent writes a bad fact, you want to fix it without losing history. A truth layer built for that keeps an immutable record of what was stored and when, so rollback is a matter of policy and tooling, not guesswork.

**Handoffs across tools.** In practice you use more than one agent surface: ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, custom MCP clients. If each one has its own memory, context does not survive the handoff. You switch tools and the new agent starts from zero or from a different copy of the world; the reasoning trail disappears. With a shared MCP-accessible store, the context and the reasoning trail survive. The same tasks, contacts, and decisions are available everywhere. That is coordination across the stack, not just within one chat. Workspace products can be the place where you view and edit; they are not the same as one neutral substrate that every tool reads and writes. The difference is architectural. One hub per product versus one store for all your AI tools.

## How I'm approaching the build

I'm dogfooding Neotoma in my own agentic setup. Email triage, task updates, execution plans, and content workflows all read and write through the same store. I define the strategy and the rules. Agents execute. I step in to approve when actions exceed my limits or when I want to correct something. The coordination is visible: I see what the agent did in the same place the next session will see it. No separate "agent memory" and "my memory." One memory, shared.

The bet is that this pattern scales. Not because every team will use my stack, but because the need for coordination scales. As more of the work we do involves agents that act, the need for a shared, legible, auditable state layer grows. Funding and enterprise adoption will follow the infrastructure that makes that coordination reliable. I'm building Neotoma to be that kind of substrate: the place where agents and humans meet, with one source of truth and one history both can rely on.
summary:
- Funding and enterprise chats both point at memory, coordination, and trust as first-class infrastructure; control planes may outlast agent frameworks.
- Neotoma does not fit AI funding lists because those measure capital consumption; it competes on epistemics and accountability, like Git or SQLite.
- Platform-owned convenience memory is a feature; user-owned, inspectable, cross-agent memory is infrastructure that resists enclosure.
- Neotoma is to agents what Git is to code: one store, one history, one provenance layer so handoffs survive across tools and sessions.
- Dogfooding the same layer for email, tasks, and content so coordination is visible and reliable.
shareTweet: Where agents and humans meet: one shared state layer, legible and auditable, so coordination survives handoffs. Why I'm building Neotoma as the truth layer. https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma

title: What if AI wasn't conversational?
excerpt: The default interface to AI is chat. That may be what pushes everyone into managing the AI. Other interfaces could keep the human in the loop without turning us all into managers.
body:
- The dominant way we use AI is conversational. We prompt, the model answers, we correct, we iterate. That pattern feels a lot like managing a junior.
- If "everyone becomes a product manager" or "everyone becomes a manager of the AI" is the future, it is partly because we adopted an interface that casts the human as the one who instructs and corrects.
- There are other ways to interface with AI. Declarative intent, outcome boundaries, persistent agency, and shared artifacts could change the unit of work so the human sets goals and constraints instead of supervising a conversation.
- The scarce skill could stay "defining what good looks like" and "where the human must stay in the loop" without that being people management.

A thread on X a few days ago stuck with me. Someone said that in practice most engineers will have to become product managers. A reply added that someone has to prompt the models, talk to customers, coordinate teams, and decide what to build next. Engineering is changing; great engineers are more important than ever, but the work looks more like directing and coordinating than writing every line of code.

I left a comment: the hardest pill for individual contributors might be that every white-collar job becomes a bit like management. Unless we get a different kind of interface to AI and agents, one that does not mimic human-to-human training and guidance.

This post is about that "unless."

## Why conversation dominates

Chat is the default because it is flexible and natural. You can ask anything. You can correct on the fly. You can iterate until the output is good enough. That flexibility is real. It is also expensive. You spend time re-explaining context, steering the model back on track, and checking that it did what you meant. The mental load is the same as managing someone who needs clear instructions and frequent feedback. The interface invites that metaphor.

When the primary interaction is a back-and-forth, the human is cast as the one who knows what to do and has to communicate it. The model is cast as the one who executes and sometimes misunderstands. That division is not fixed by technology. It is reinforced by the shape of the interface.

## What a non-conversational interface could be

I am not arguing for removing conversation entirely. I am asking what would change if we had interfaces that did not default to "tell the AI what to do in natural language, then fix what it got wrong."

**Declarative intent.** You specify what should hold: goals, invariants, "do not do X." The system figures out how. You edit constraints; you do not write step-by-step instructions. The unit of work is "here is the contract; satisfy it," not "do A, then B, then C."

**Outcome and boundary as controls.** The main levers are "this is in scope," "this is out of scope," "this is the definition of done," "revert if tests fail." You set boundaries and success criteria. The agent executes and only pulls you in when it is stuck or when a boundary is violated. The feel is configuring a system, not directing a subordinate.

**Agent as persistent counterpart.** The agent has memory and continuity across sessions. It can say "last time you preferred X" or "you usually want Y here; confirm?" You are not re-explaining or re-training every time. You confirm or correct, but you are not redoing the whole brief.

**Structured handoffs.** Instead of open-ended chat, the agent has clear states: "I am good," "I need a decision on X," "I am blocked on Y." You step in only at decision or block points. The interface could be a list of pending decisions with options, not a long thread you have to read and manage.

**Tool-shaped interfaces.** For well-scoped tasks, the interface is a tool with a few knobs. "Run this kind of analysis." "Draft this type of document." The agent runs the tool; you do not prompt it through the steps. Conversation is for the open-ended cases, not for every task.

**Shared artifacts as source of truth.** The main object of interaction is a shared artifact: a doc, a spec, a plan. You change the artifact; the agent interprets and executes. The canonical instructions live in the artifact. You are not "training" the agent in chat; you are editing the thing that both of you treat as truth.

In each case, the human sets goals, boundaries, or criteria. The system handles execution and escalates only at defined points. That is a different division of labor than "human manages the AI in an ongoing conversation."

## Why it matters for ICs

If the only interface we get is conversational, then the only way to get good results is to get good at directing the conversation. That skill set overlaps heavily with management: clarity of intent, prioritization, feedback loops, quality control. Individual contributors who prefer to focus on depth of execution would still have to spend a large share of their time in that managerial mode.

If we get interfaces where the human operates on goals and boundaries rather than on every turn of the dialogue, the scarce skill stays "what good looks like" and "where the human must remain in the loop." That can stay an IC discipline. You can be the person who defines the contract, the success criteria, and the escalation rules without being the person who manages a team. The role is more like product or design than like people management.

I do not know which interfaces will win or how fast. I do think the question "what if AI wasn't conversational?" is worth taking seriously. The answer could determine whether the future of white-collar work feels like everyone managing the AI or like everyone configuring and steering systems that run with less conversation and more structure.
summary:
- Chat casts the human as instructor and corrector, which mirrors management and may be why "everyone becomes a PM" feels plausible.
- Non-conversational interfaces could include declarative intent, outcome boundaries, persistent agent memory, and shared artifacts as source of truth.
- In those models the human sets goals and constraints; the system executes and escalates only at defined decision or block points.
- The scarce skill could stay "defining what good looks like" without becoming people management.
- The question "what if AI wasn't conversational?" could shape whether white-collar work feels like managing the AI or like configuring and steering systems.
shareTweet: What if AI wasn't conversational? Chat makes us the instructor and corrector. Other interfaces (declarative intent, boundaries, shared artifacts) could keep us in the loop without turning us all into managers of the AI.

title: OpenAI API memory: What exists, what's missing, and when to add a truth layer
excerpt: The OpenAI API has conversation state and file search, but no first-class memory API. Here's what developers get, what they don't, and when a truth layer fills the gap.
body:
OpenAI's developer API gives you conversation state and retrieval over files. It does not give you a dedicated memory API. Unlike [Anthropic's Claude Memory Tool](/posts/claude-memory-and-the-truth-layer)—where Claude reads and writes files in a `/memories` directory and you implement the backend—OpenAI has no equivalent. You get conversations, optional file search (vector stores), and the responsibility to persist and inject user context yourself. This post explains what exists, what's missing, and when a [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) is a better fit. For the product-level experience (ChatGPT Memory), see [ChatGPT Memory](/posts/chatgpt-memory-and-truth-layer).

## What the OpenAI API provides

**Conversation state.** The [Responses API](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/responses-vs-chat-completions) (OpenAI's evolution of Chat Completions and the Assistants API) supports multi-turn context in two ways. You can pass message history manually with each request, or use the [Conversations API](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/conversation-state) to create a durable conversation object with a stable ID. Conversations store items—messages, tool calls, tool outputs—so you don't have to resend full history every turn. State is preserved across sessions and devices for that conversation. The `store: true` parameter keeps reasoning and tool context between turns. So: **conversation memory exists; user-level or cross-conversation memory does not.**

**File search (vector stores).** You can attach [vector stores](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-file-search) to assistants or threads (and in the new model, to prompts and conversations). Files are chunked, embedded with `text-embedding-3-large`, and retrieved by semantic and keyword search at query time. This is RAG over a knowledge base—documents, docs, codebase—not a place for the model to write user-specific memories. Retrieval is hosted by OpenAI (e.g. [pricing](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/tools-file-search) around storage and usage). The same [limits that RAG has for agent memory](/posts/why-agent-memory-needs-more-than-rag) apply: no canonical identity, no provenance, similarity-driven retrieval, no schema-driven queries.

**Your backend.** OpenAI's own [state management guidance](https://developers.openai.com/apps-sdk/build/storage/) is clear: long-lived business data, cross-session state, and user-specific context belong in *your* backend or MCP server. You persist preferences, user facts, and session metadata; you inject them into the conversation (e.g. system message or initial user message) so the model has context. The API does not read or write a dedicated memory store. You build that layer yourself.

## What's missing

**No first-class memory API.** There is no opt-in tool type that lets the model read and write a persistent memory store the way Claude's Memory Tool does. So there is no built-in flow for "the model decides what to remember and writes it; next session it reads it." You must either (1) keep everything in conversation history (and hit context limits or truncation), (2) use file search for static documents only, or (3) maintain your own store and inject summaries or structured context into each request. All of that is custom.

**No client-side memory backend.** With Claude's Memory Tool, you implement file handlers and control where data lives (including Zero Data Retention). With OpenAI, conversation state and vector stores are server-side. [Data controls](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/your-data) (e.g. Zero Data Retention, modified abuse monitoring) affect retention of API data; they don't give you a client-held memory store that the model writes to via tool calls.

**Context and cost.** The more you put into the system message or early turns to represent "what we know about this user," the more tokens you burn and the less room for the actual dialogue. Truncation and summarization are on you. There is no standard way to say "load only the memories relevant to this turn."

## Where the current setup works

- **Single-conversation apps.** If your product is one long-running conversation (or a small number of them), the Conversations API plus manual or injected context is enough. You can store a small amount of user context in your DB and prepend it each time.
- **Document Q&A.** File search over uploaded docs, code, or knowledge bases works for retrieval-augmented Q&A. Not for user memory.
- **Stateless or short-lived agents.** When you don't need persistent user-level memory across many conversations, the API is sufficient.

## Where a truth layer fits

When you need **the same facts** to be consistent across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or other tools, or when you need **provenance, rollback, or deterministic queries**, the API's conversation state and file search don't provide that. I've written about [similar limits with retrieval alone](/posts/agentic-search-and-the-truth-layer): no canonical identity, no audit trail, non-deterministic answers. A [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) gives you typed entities, stable IDs, lineage, and cross-platform access via MCP. You keep using the OpenAI API for inference and conversation; the truth layer holds the state that must be shared, queryable, and auditable.

**When to use the API as-is.** You're building an OpenAI-only product, context is mostly per-conversation or you're okay injecting a small amount of user context from your own DB, and you don't need cross-tool memory or full auditability.

**When to add a truth layer.** You need the same state in multiple agents or tools, complete recall over structured data, traceability of where an answer came from, or rollback when the model writes something wrong. Then the API handles the chat; the truth layer handles the memory.

## How configuration works (and doesn't)

With the Responses API, you create a conversation (or pass history), send input items, and get output items. You can attach file-search vector stores to the request. There is no "memory" tool to configure. To simulate persistent user memory, you (1) store user context in your backend, (2) load it when starting or continuing a conversation, and (3) include it in the prompt or early messages. How you store and load it—database, key-value store, or a [truth layer like Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma)—is up to you. Neotoma can be the backend for that context; the model never calls it directly. Your app fetches from Neotoma (e.g. via MCP or your API) and injects the result into the OpenAI request.

## What I'm building

I'm building [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) as a structured memory layer: typed entities, canonical IDs, provenance, and cross-platform access via MCP. It doesn't replace the OpenAI API; it sits underneath it. You use the API for conversation and reasoning; you use Neotoma (or another truth layer) when you need memory that is stable, queryable, and portable across all the agents and tools in your stack.
summary:
- The OpenAI API has conversation state (Responses API, Conversations API) and file search (vector stores), but no first-class memory API—unlike Claude's Memory Tool.
- You persist and inject user context yourself; the API doesn't read or write a dedicated memory store. File search is RAG over documents, not user memory.
- Where the current setup works: single-conversation apps, document Q&A, stateless or short-lived agents.
- When a truth layer fits: you need the same facts across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, or other tools; provenance; rollback; or deterministic queries. The API handles chat; the truth layer handles memory.
shareTweet: OpenAI API has conversation state and file search—but no first-class memory API. What developers get, what's missing, and when a truth layer fits. https://markmhendrickson.com/posts/openai-api-memory-and-truth-layer

title: Notion for AI agents: Where it fits and when to add a truth layer
excerpt: If you use Notion with AI agents or Notion MCP, this post explains where Notion excels, where it hits limits, and when adding a truth layer like Neotoma makes sense.
body:
[Akshay Kothari](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/), Notion's COO, recently wrote that agents need full context to do their best work: the artifacts humans create and the data our systems generate. Humans need to inspect, evaluate, edit, and approve what agents produce, in a format we can understand. In a world where humans and agents collaborate, both sides need to be legible to each other. Notion, he argues, is in a position to provide that infrastructure.

I agree with the framing. Legibility is the right word. The missing piece in a lot of agent builds is not raw capability but a shared surface where context lives and where humans can see what the agent did and change it. If you are already using Notion for AI agents or [Notion MCP](https://developers.notion.com/docs/mcp), this post is for you: what Notion is offering as agentic infrastructure, where it excels, where it hits limits, and when adding a [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) like [Neotoma](https://github.com/markmhendrickson/neotoma) makes sense. The two can sit next to each other; you do not have to choose one or the other.

## What Notion is offering

Notion is a workspace: docs, databases, wikis, project trackers, and now a layer that agents can read and write via [MCP](https://developers.notion.com/docs/mcp) and other integrations. The product stores the artifacts humans create (notes, specs, runbooks, CRM rows) and can receive output from agents (drafts, task updates, summaries). Because everything is blocks and databases in one system, agents get a single context surface. Because the same content is editable by humans in a familiar UI, humans get a single place to inspect and correct what agents did.

So the infrastructure claim is: we host the context; we make it legible; we make it editable. That is a real and valuable role. It is not the same as "we are the memory layer." It is "we are the place where context and legibility meet."

## Where Notion excels

**One place for human and agent work.** Teams already keep specs, meeting notes, and project state in Notion. Letting agents read and write the same pages and databases means context does not have to be copied into a separate agent store. The agent operates on the same objects the team already looks at. That reduces the risk of agents and humans working from different copies of the truth.

**Legibility by design.** Notion’s surface is documents and structured databases, not opaque embeddings or session-only state. You can open a page and see what the agent wrote. You can edit it, comment, or revert. The format is one humans already use. That fits Akshay’s point: both sides need to be legible to each other. [Alexis Kouchev added](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) that capability only matters if humans can verify what the agent did and why, and raised the harder problem: maintaining that context across handoffs between different tools, where the reasoning trail disappears. A shared context layer that agents and humans both use helps with the first; keeping that layer consistent across ChatGPT, Claude, Cursor, and other tools is where a dedicated memory substrate pays off.

**Collaboration and handoff.** When multiple people (or people and agents) need to work on the same content, a shared workspace helps. Notion supports that. So does the idea of "somewhere between Notion and Slack" that [Niel Robertson raised](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) in the comments: a place for inputs, structured outputs, and communication. Notion is already that place for many teams.

**Richness of context.** [Dr. Xi Zeng’s comment](https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7430484741340033024/) that "the bottleneck isn’t intelligence—it’s context richness" and that "the teams that build the best context graphs will have agents that outperform those with 10x more compute" lines up with this. Notion holds a lot of that context. Teams that already live in Notion get a lot of value from exposing it to agents without rebuilding a separate knowledge layer.

## What’s missing from a state perspective

Notion is optimized for documents and collaboration. It is not optimized for the kind of state that needs canonical identity, full provenance, and deterministic querying. Those are different requirements.

**No canonical entity identity across sources.** Notion has unique page and database row IDs inside Notion. It does not resolve "the same person" or "the same company" across an email thread, a PDF you upload, and a row an agent created. If the agent writes "Acme Corp" in one page and "ACME CORPORATION" in another, there is no built-in merge or canonical ID. A [truth layer](/posts/truth-layer-agent-memory) built for agent memory does entity resolution and stable IDs so that "all documents involving vendor X" is a single, unambiguous set.

**Provenance is at the page level, not the fact level.** You can see who edited a block and when. You cannot trace "this number came from this email, this session, this inference" in a way that survives aggregation and reuse. For advisory use that is often enough.
